- These experiences are
often called 'ineffable,' but as a scientist, I do my
best to 'F- it up' by trying to describe it as much as I can, so I'll go ahead and do that: Our society has been in
a mental health crisis and it's been stagnating. A large portion of people treat it with traditional antidepressants,
don't see benefit. Psychedelics are the only
drug class that I know of where you have not just one or two, but lots of stories of people saying they took this thing one time and they say it had this profound impact on the course of their entire life. They really exploded on
the scene in the 1960s and were associated with some really radical
changes to society. That really scared a lot of people. I think now society is more ready to kind of accept these compounds and the whole picture of them. I'm Matt Johnson. I'm a Professor of
Psychiatry at Johns Hopkins. I study drugs, principally, psychedelics. It's hard to imagine a more
interdisciplinary topic in the realm of drugs than psychedelics. They've apparently been
used for time immemorial throughout many different
cultures on the planet. Kary Mullis won the Nobel
Prize inventing PCR, which revolutionized biology. He said he wouldn't have
been able to do that had he not had experience
in taking psychedelics. You move into the arts
and then, I mean, gosh, I mean who hasn't heard of musicians and other artists
claiming that psychedelics had a profound impact on their creativity? There's the Beatles before LSD, and then there's the Beatles after LSD- that's a big shift. The initial big wave of
that scientific interest for psychedelics was in the 1950s. So this was about a decade
after Albert Hofmann had first discovered the
psychoactive properties of LSD. There was a group in Maryland called the "Spring Grove Group." A lot of early research there, particularly with cancer patients. But I would say one of
the most important groups in the history of this work was led by Abram Hoffer and Humphrey
Osmond in Saskatchewan, Canada. They were really the first group that I would say developed what we now call "psychedelic therapy." There was this growing
appreciation of some of the risks and the safeguards needed
to minimize those risks, but not every investigator
was aware of this. There was some unethical
research, some wild research. There was controversy
surrounding Timothy Leary, especially after he
was fired from Harvard. - 'Turn on, tune in, and drop out.' - Psychedelics became so
associated with the counterculture. Society was in a sense, traumatized. It all came to a halt. I mean, there were researchers
that were running sessions and got the notices they just had to stop. The rug is pulled out for decades before there's a reinitiation. The research that's happening now, a lot of it is exploring
medical applications. These are all drugs that
can have a profound effect on one's sense of reality,
including one's sense of self. I think kind of the
only bet you can make is that if you have the dose high enough, something really
interesting is gonna happen. You might find yourself
in your 'mind's eye,' soaring in the heavens; the secrets of the Universe
seemingly revealed to you. On the other hand, five seconds later it might collapse into what
folks call a 'bad trip,' where one could feel
that they're gonna die. It's sort of this like massive shake up in one's experience of reality. I'm floored when people with really substantial life experience will say things like, 'This was the most meaningful
experience of their life.' Compared to the intensity
of the subjective effects, it's remarkable how little impact these substances have on physiology. You know, your heart
rate, your blood pressure- everything has its risk, to be clear. But compared to other drug classes, psychedelics are really robustly safe at the physiological level. My therapeutic research with
psychedelics is primarily used 'psilocybin,' which is the
agent in magic mushrooms. The dose we now give
to patients is anywhere from 30 milligrams to 40 milligrams, which Terence McKenna, who's
the famous psychedelic bard would refer to repeatedly
as the "heroic dose." If a microdose is over here and a recreational dose is
over here, we're way over here. When someone comes in for the day of their psychedelic
session, they've already spent several hours with us
on previous days preparing for the experience,
developing that rapport. We know that all drugs are
in part shaped by the context in which they're given, but
for the psychedelic substances, that's particularly more so. So when you provide it in more of a warm, welcoming situation, you tend to get more meaningful
reactions to the substance. When the experience unfolds the person is gonna lay
down, wear eye shades, and headphones through
which music is played. So the idea is: kinda get out of your normal, everyday,
discursive analyzing intellect. We really just encourage them
to trust, let go, and be open. Trust the overall process. Going into this type of
treatment is not easy at all. Trust yourself that you're robust: You're gonna get through this experience. Let go. What does let go mean? Let go means whatever
your preconceived notions of what this experience might be or what experience you should have, don't let that get in the
way for the experience that you will have. Don't try to control it, and be open no matter where this takes you, go there. Whatever the experience is,
take the orientation of: 'This is what I need to deal with.' The range of experiences
are just so broad. You can swing from a terrifying experience to this beautific, you know, sort of like, most amazing
experience of your life. Some people could feel like
they've reached the end of reality, this kind
of plane of existence. Others say that they
can feel the suffering across the entire planet. You know, sort of having
this sort of experience of 'universal empathy.' It's all welcome. The laughter, the tears,
everything in between. That's exactly what this process is for. So the 'integration phase'
is where we're discussing the experience with them, and the themes that come up are so varied. One of the aspects you
could call 'revealed truth,' and I call these 'duh moments.' These are experiences
where people will feel like they've learned something
at such a fundamental level. I think of examples in our work we've done with cancer patients where, you know, oftentimes people will say, "I'm allowing this to
create my own suffering. The cancer hasn't killed me yet. I can still live life, but
I'm letting it do this to me." They knew it up here, they
could tell you the words, but now they feel it in their bones- they feel it in their heart. People are reminded about this power they have to choose their own destiny. Some people come out of these
sessions as participants and say that, you know, "One 6-hour experience with psilocybin is like a
thousand hours of therapy." But one of the pitfalls
of this clinical work is that you'd be hard pressed
to find a more intimate and vulnerable psychological
intervention for a person. That's just a situation
that is ripe for abuses if we don't have the right safeguards. Because of that vulnerability, because of the incredible
gravity of these sessions, we need to even more tenaciously cling to clinical boundaries. We need to limit the use
of therapeutic touch. A subtler form of abuse is just filling in the blanks for people- I call it the guru complex. People often confront
big picture questions: What is the meaning of life? Does God exist? What's the big picture of
everything that's going on? What am I? On those metaphysical questions, we really have to be silent. In the biggest picture, aside
from psychedelic's ability to treat one disorder or another, I think they're just
poised to really help us to understand the nature
of psychiatric disorders. What commonalities do
they have with each other that are somehow being addressed
by psychedelic treatment? I kind of think of all of these disorders as different forms of addiction. So whether it's to a substance
like tobacco or alcohol, cocaine, or a way of
thinking about yourself, it's this narrowed mental
and behavioral repertoire: reality used to be like this, and now your reality is like this. And psychedelics, when done
well in the right setting, have the ability to just blow people out of that narrowed story. It holds a lot of promise
in helping us figure out what it takes for the human
being to be mentally healthy. - Get smarter, faster, with videos from the world's biggest thinkers. To learn even more from the
world's biggest thinkers, get Big Think+ for your business.

helping the researchers get to that point. And I don't want to fuck that up. (Laughter) So, the other important difference
with cannabis, is there was a real social justice reason to decriminalize or legalize cannabis and that was cannabis prosecution was at the heart of the drug war. There are hundreds of thousands, 
if not millions of people, many of them African Americans,  who are in jail because of the 3 strikes one of those strikes, very often 
was a cannabis crime it gave the police a very powerful tool

in our culture where we're not supposed to take things
that change our consciousness but it's ok for all of us to be 
on antidepressants. Why? Why is that? MICHAEL: Well, I mean if you do a typology of drugs
that are legal and drugs that are illegal, it really doesn't make a lot of sense. Psychedelics are actually some of the most 
non-toxic drugs you can take, believe it or not. There's no lethal dose 
for psilocybin or LSD. You can't say that about many 
of the drugs in your medicine cabinet Tylenol has a lethal dose
measured in a couple dozen pills. So, why is it there? Well, it's very threatening, I think. It was very threatening in the 60's 
to the Nixon administration Nixon thought that it was LSD 
that was sapping the will of American boys to go off and fight. He may have been partly right, 
it caused people to question, you know, for most of human history, 18 year old boys march off to war
when you tell them to go, and suddenly that wasn't happening. That was pretty unprecedented. And it was also threatening, you know, there was a huge generation gap
in the 60's and I think psychedelics had something 
to do with that in that the young were having
a right of passage, and the LSD trip is a right of passage, that the old were not familiar with and I think that was very scary. ALISON: Just a minute ago you were
saying there's a social justice dimension to legalizing marijuana because of the way that it was used 
from Nixon on, even before that, to target specific populations. To target the anti-war movement... MICHAEL: Well, John Ehrlichman actually 
let the cat out of the bag he gave a quote in Harpers Magazine that just surfaced 
in the last couple years but he gave the quote I think
back in the 80's, and he said, "we needed the drug war 
to go after the blacks and the hippies." I mean, you can't be more blunt than that and pot was the way to 
go after both of them and psychedelics was the way to 
go after the hippies, by legalizing. ALISON: Do you think though, that it was a kind of anti-youth
move in general I mean, you talked about the war and obviously there were all these other factors that led 
to the anti-war movement. MICHAEL: And the Baby Boom too, you had this huge bulge, 
demographic bulge of young people that changed the culture. So that was part of it, but I do think it was destabilizing
in certain ways. I mean, think about it, the more distance we get on the 60's
the odder they are you had a youth culture that withdrew from adult culture
in certain ways, and it had its own ways of speaking, it had its own ways of dressing,
and mores, and manners It was trying to kind of,
be as different as it could be and it had its own drugs, and adults had their 
"mother's little helper" that Rolling Stones talked about they had tranquilizers and alcohol
they had depressants, and the young had psychedelics. ALISON: Little did we know how bad
the "mummy's helper" was going to be I mean Valium was probably
a much more dangerous drug in some ways. MICHAEL: Yeah, but, 
going back to this idea of why are some drugs legal and illegal some lubricate society, 
I mean, coffee right? ALISON: Make it work. MICHAEL: They help people work, 
they help people socialize, you know, alcohol in small amounts, 
is a great social lubricant the drugs that really disrupt things
are the ones that often get banned, and when caffeine first showed up,
there were efforts to make that illegal, and then everybody realized, you can't do that, it's too helpful, it's helping people stay up later, 
work harder, you know, capitalism likes this drug, and capitalism is figuring out how to 
like psychedelics now, with microdosing, of course. That's the way to make it an acceptable 
capitalistic drug, cause microdosing is essentially taking
this incredibly Dionysian disruptive drug and turning it into another
productivity drug, it's kinda perfect. (Laughter) ALISON: I wanted to ask you about 
your relationship to the counter culture I mean, you didn't live it, 
but there's a real tension in this book around exactly the Dionysian dimension
of LSD or psychedelic mushrooms that people were tapping in to. And that is seen as threatening,  and you can talk a lot
about American mores and all of that and I know this book is an effort
in a way, to kind of pull off all of that
psychedelic draping but what is your relationship
to the counter culture, do you see that as a legitimate 
hangover of LSD? Do you think that there was something
that literally derailed LSD research? Do you have an ambivalence about that? MICHAEL: I mean, you know, a lot of things went south after the 60's and you can blame the 60's counter culture
for all sorts of things, but I tend to stress the positive things, I think it gave us a lot of what 
we really prize, or at least what a lot of us really prize, such as the environmental movement, women's liberation,
civil rights to some extent, these are products of the 60's. And we're still fighting about them, the amazing thing is that, I just didn't think that 
at this point in my life, the terms of debate 
that were set in the 60's around personal liberation, 
and questioning authority, and all the kind of things, 
we would still be arguing about. I mean, we are still in the backlash 
to the 60's, the modern right, is all about 
a backlash to the 60's, abortion is a classic case. And I find it very sad 
that we're still having that battle, and I realize it's going to consume
the rest of my life, and I thought actually when Obama
was elected in that moment in Grant Park when the family came out, and I said this, 
I said this to Judith, that maybe we've finally turned the page and something new is gonna start. Didn't happen. So a lot of interesting historical 
political forces were set in motion then and they continue to reverberate 
in our culture and so that psychedelics should
return now, in a way, is kind of a return 
of the repressed, I mean, that they're coming back
into our society, has to do with the fact
that they did have value, and people committed to them,
kept the flame alive, it was like a dark age, and really the flame was held 
at Esalen, this new age conference center
in California where they never stopped talking 
about psychedelics, and I'm guessing using them, and then when things were ripe enough, and you could restart the research, and the FDA allowed them to restart 
the research, it's sort of reflowering. And then you have the fact that 
the tech community, which wasn't true in the 60's, there really wasn't
a tech community in that sense, became so interested in them, and that had to do with Burning Man, which is a kind of neo 60's event, 
I gather, I've never been. but that has cultivated a lot of interest 
in psychedelics and frankly the fact that 
we have a mental health crisis. One of the big surprises to me 
in doing this book was I thought psychiatry 
and the mental health world would be very critical 
and there would be a lot of pushback it's been the opposite, that they are so receptive, people who have looked at the question, because they are in desperate 
need of new tools, and there's a general recognition 
in psychiatry, that the pharmacological tools
that they have are really not adequate they mostly treat symptoms, 
they have terrible side-effects they're addictive, very hard to get off, and people don't like taking them. And so, if you compare mental healthcare
with any other branch of medicine, whether it's oncology, cardiology,
infectious disease, have they reduced suffering
and saved lives at the same rate? I don't think so. It's a harder problem, but that I think is fueling 
a lot of the interest, essentially a sense of desperation and that if we have a tool 
that can really help people not just address symptoms, 
but actually cure mental disorders, we can't turn away from that. ALISON: You said earlier that by 
writing this book and then publishing op-eds as things are happening
around psychedelics you've developed a sense of responsibility
for what happens and I'm not saying you're like 
Timothy Leary although, do you feel any 
connection to him? Not obviously in his sort of vision, but as someone who kind of really brought this into
broad public consciousness MICHAEL: I mean there's 
a lot of cautionary lessons in the Timothy Leary story. He became an evangelist 
for psychedelics, he wanted simply to give them 
to as many people as possible, he really thought, he calculated, 
if 4 million Americans took LSD we could change the society, in fact only 2 million had 
by the end of the 60's, and they did change the society, 
but not exactly the way he thought. No, I don't identify with him at all, I came to psychedelics very late in life my experience is much more limited, I don't know how many trips he had,
but it was in the hundreds, and he became sort of a cult leader, 
in certain ways, and I don't have a cult behind me. ALISON: Come on. (Laughter) MICHAEL: I do feel 
a sense of responsibility because I have the culture's ear 
on this subject right now as I did with food a few years ago, and once that's true, 
you have to think a little bit beyond your own curiosity and your own
interest in personal growth to what effect might what you're
writing and saying have on other people and people will write me all the time
and tell me that they read the book, and they went out and tried this or that and I always get a little nervous. How'd it go? Are you ok? And sometimes they're not ok, sometimes you hear about kind of scary
stories people have. So, I do feel a little 
sense of responsibility, and that's why I'm always sure to 
talk to people about risk as well as benefits. ALISON: Well, not to put 
you on the spot, but given that you are in this position, one of the first questions that we got, so I have my questions, but many of you submitted questions, 
in advance of the program, and thank you for those. So, I'm gonna weave a few of those
into this conversation, and the first one comes from Nick, and I think goes back to where we started
with the op-ed, and he says "let's say you were somehow given broad
legislative authority in the United States on this topic, how would you approach
some of it's details, and what would the end state of affairs look like?" MICHAEL: Great question. And the answer is I'm not sure exactly. Basically, here's how I see 
the future of psychedelics we have this medical model, this medicalization container, and the way that's going to work, assuming it's approved, which I think will happen in the next 
5 to 7 years, say, that working with an MD, 
or probably a psychiatrist, you will be able to get 
a psychedelic session, if you're depressed,
if you perhaps have a cancer diagnosis, that was very successful 
trial the happened, if you have anxiety, 
and if you're an addict, and what that will consist of, and this seems to be the model 
that is developing, is a guided psychedelic trip, where you work with two therapist, 
a man and a woman, very intensely, but for a very short
amount of time, and so they prepare you very carefully
for the experience, they also qualify you for it, they make sure you're not at risk
of schizophrenia, that's disqualifying, 
if you have that in your family history, and if you're on certain
psychiatric medicines, they don't want you, 
partly for drug interaction, but also because SSRI's blunt
the effect of psychedelics, and getting off of SSRI's
is risky as it is and then they also tell you
what to do if you have a bad trip, or what they would call 
a challenging trip. And that if you're really frightened,
go to what's frightening you, if you feel like you're going crazy, or your mind's dissolving,
or you see a monster, go with it, surrender,
that's the key thing relax your mind and float downstream, 
as John Lennon advised us. And then during the experience, 
they sit with you the whole time, and they don't say very much, they're not trying to direct 
the experience, but they're there to hold your hand 
if you're getting really upset, or help you get up and go to the bathroom,
or whatever, give you a sip of water, and then afterwards, you come back
a day later, or two days later, and you have what's called 
an integration session where they help you make sense
of what happened. So that's the medical model, 
as it's evolving, and it seems to be very 
effective for those indications. So, that's one side, ok, so for people with mental illness, 
and dealing with crisis, we have that. On the other end, 
we have a religious model, you know, cultures have been 
using psychedelics for thousands of years and some of them still are, so you have the ayahuasca model
which comes from the Amazon, it's a very powerful psychedelic
DMT is the drug, but it's a tea made from 
several different plants and there are two ayahuasca churches
in America, that have chapters all over the country they're not that well know 
and if you join that church they administer ayahuasca as a sacriment, and they're legally entitled to do that under separation of church and state,
and something called The Religious Freedom Restoration Act that was passed under 
the Clinton Administration. And the Native American Church, also which is an indigenous,
it's a multi-tribe church that I think about 150 American tribes
are a part of now, and they can use peyote 
in their ceremonies, legally. So you have this religious model, you have this medical model, and then there's the rest of us, who perhaps are not suffering
from a mental illness diagnosis, are not in these churches. What one of my sources 
immortally referred to as "The betterment of well people" which I think is an 
awkwardly lovely phrase. And, so what about us? How will we get access? And that's the hard part, I don't know exactly 
how to design that. Now, there is going to be a 
ballot initiative in Oregon that will take a stab at this. Two psychologists started this, and they're basically, 
they designed a regime, for the state health authorities, If it passes, to train therapists, most of whom are now working illegally to give them a certain kind of training, 
and an exam, and a license, and they will be allowed
to give psilocybin to anyone they want to, 
anyone who wants to take it, they don't have to have a diagnosis, 
it won't be covered by insurance, and we'll see. I mean, is that the best 
way to design this? Basically we have this
really interesting cultural project that I don't know the answer to which is, how do we 
create a container for people to use this in a way that's safe
and won't be subject to the kinds of things that happen
underground very often. So, I'm curious about that, but the other thing I could easily 
imagine happening and this is not saying I'd design it,
but it will happen, is that there will be clinics and they'll be a little like spas, and they'll have a psychiatrist on staff, they'll be like mental health clubs, 
and you will go there, and if you're qualified, 
they will give you a guided trip, and the psychiatrist will 
write the prescription. I just talked to a group in Canada, that wants to design those 
clinics right now. ALISON: I guess that leads
to the question which comes back to social justice, in that vision, or even in the stuff
you were documenting writing this book, are the right people getting access? Will the right people get access? Or does this become a kind of
rich person's elite experience, because that even happened
in the first wave, like the drug company was giving tons
of LSD to all these researchers who barely had to be qualified to get it, but then they would do
these trials with it, or facilitate people's experiences, 
and charge like 500 bucks. MICHAEL: Yeah, and more now. So, the virtue, I mean one of
the considerable virtues of going down this legalization path is that it will, once it's approved
by the FDA, and rescheduled by the DEA it will be covered by insurance, so people presumably
with health insurance, with medicaid, will have access to it. So in a way, the medical model 
democratizes it most effectively. These spas, or these clinics, 
will be I'm sure expensive. Right now it's interesting, though, I mean one of the things I explore 
in the book is this underground, which is an informal
network of very serious therapists who are practicing illegally
because they believe so strongly in this and they actually have a code of conduct which I read, you can find it online, and part of that is, that you charge 
people what they can afford, so they overcharge affluent people, 
and they undercharge people who are poor. That said, though, the psychedelic world
is pretty white and pretty affluent, and I was doing an event like this 
the other night with an African American writer, 
and psychonaut, very rare, he founded
The People of Color Camp at Burning Man which I didn't even know existed, and we had an interesting conversation
about why that should be so and what came out of it 
was a couple things, which was that the African American
community has very conservative attitudes
towards drugs in general, and that they've seen crack cocaine, 
and heroine wreck their communities, ALISON: And the criminalization, right? MICHAEL: Right, and how drugs were
used as a tool of oppression in African American communities. So there's a real skepticism and then you have the legacy 
of Tuskegee and the medical experiments performed on African Americans and there's a story I tell in the book,
it's actually just a footnote, that Timothy Leary, 
one of his experiments, he wanted to give it to hardened criminals
at the Concord State Prison, to see if he could lower
the recidivism rate, and in those days, avoiding the 
Tuskegee image I guess, he said, well the researchers will take 
the drugs with the prisoners, so they're not treated like guinea pigs.
It was a wild time. And he's sitting there with this 
African American who was a murderer or rapist or something,
a very tough character, and he's really nervous, 
cause what's this guy gonna do when he goes off on his trip, and he says to the guy
"I'm really nervous" and the prisoner said
" well I'm nervous too." And he said "well why are you nervous?" and the prisoner said "cause you're a 
fucking mad scientist." (Laughter) But the other reason I think
that came out of this conversation, was that to use psychedelics, Leary said,
and this was his big contribution, set and setting are very important. You have to be in an environment
where you feel maximally safe in your body to let your mind go there, 
go as far away as it's gonna go, and a great many African Americans
don't feel that, they feel alert, and the need to be alert, 
because of encounters with the police, and not having an environment where
you could let go for 6 or 8 hours and that I think too, has 
made it a pretty unappealing drug in that community. ALISON: So we're 40 minutes in,
and I have to segue to the part of the program, 
I'm going to call Michael Pollan on Drugs cause I know people
want to talk about this so I have a couple of questions, and then I'm going to ask questions, 
from people who submitted them. So, my question, is just what
was your favorite part of getting high? MICHAEL: You know, I don't think
of it as getting high, I mean, that word just seems more 
appropriate to cannabis, or alcohol. ALISON: Tripping? MICHAEL: Tripping, journeying, I think
that's better, you really do go places,
you don't feel high necessarily, it's just much more complicated than that. Favorite part? When it's all over. I really enjoyed the end 
of the experiences, you know, it's like safely returning home,
but with all this new information. So one of my favorite parts after these
experiences, was coming home and Judith, my wife, 
and I would have a dinner and I would tell her the whole story 
of what happened, and she would help me make sense of it, and that was kind of a highlight. But there were definite moments 
of incredible power and emotion, mostly on psilocybin, I would say
I had my best experiences on psilocybin, it doesn't mean I couldn't have 
had better experiences on LSD or ayahuasca but for one reason or another 
the circumstance was just right, and I guess the happiest experience
was an unguided psilocybin trip, at our home in New England, and walking through my garden
and seeing it with new eyes. One of the really interesting things
that happens on psychedelics is everything feels alive,
much more alive than it ever has, inanimate things can feel alive, 
but plants to me especially, and you know, we were talking about 
an earlier book of mine Botany of Desire the subtitle is
A Plant's-Eye View of The World and the premise of it, 
or the intellectual conceit is that the domesticated plants 
manipulate us, even as we manipulate them That they have their own subjectivity, 
or agency in evolution. And, I understood that 
idea intellectually, but during this trip I felt it. I felt the plants returning my gaze, 
I felt them being conscious beings, not like us, they weren't talking, 
or anything but that they had their own awareness 
of the environment their own agency, their own subjectivity, we go through life thinking we are 
the only thinking subject, and maybe there's a handful of people 
we accord that privilege to in our lives we tend to objectify, though, even people but we definitely objectify nature and it's a great power, it's ego, it's the ego's power
to objectify the other. Because once you've
objectified the other, you can exploit it, you can make use of it
you can be instrumental about it, and on psychedelics, everything 
is subjective everything has a subjectivity. And it was a beautiful moment, and I walked out to 
this building that I built that was the subject of my second book
A Place of My Own and I sat at the desk there, 
and I was looking out, and there were these two trees, I had sited the building 
to frame these two trees one was this very elegant white oak
and the other was this ash tree that was sort of beat up and had lost 
a lot of limbs, and was stout and I realized for the first time, oh, that's my mother 
and the that's my father and their spirit was in those trees and at the time they were both still alive but it was so vivid,
like why didn't you know that before and that tree had my mother's elegance, and she's kind of thin, and long arms and the beat up old ash tree was 
very much like my dad who at the time was ill. So I was kind of communing with them and then made my way back through 
the garden and I never felt more a part of nature,
than I did on that walk. I mean I think most of us feel 
that we're slightly removed even when we're having a powerful
nature experience we're not in it entirely, 
we have one foot in. ALISON: And you've had many many 
powerful nature experiences. MICHAEL: I have, I have, and I feel strongly connected, 
especially to plants, but here, we speak of our relationship 
to nature and just, even that word betrays the fact
that we're not in it, right? We feel like we're part out of it, to have a relationship with something
you can't be it, and that all went away, I was completely part and parcel 
of that scene, I was one species among many, and all the species were being 
incredibly nice to me, they were very benign,
they liked me, of course, I had planted them, 
and taken care of them, they owed me. (Laughter) So that was kind of the most 
ecstatic experience. ALISON: Eric Garten asks having experienced multiple psychedelics, you kind of answered this, but are you inclined 
to repeat any of them? If yes, which ones and why?
And if not, why? MICHAEL: Well, I hasten to point out, 
it's illegal and I have been very open about my use and therefore somewhat legally vulnerable so I have not done anything since the book I look forward to the day when I can again
but I've been very careful and if they were decriminalized, 
if there was some regime, I could easily see doing psilocybin again maybe experimenting with a higher 
dose of LSD, the thing about LSD that sort of scares me
is what people say about it, is it's amazing for 6 hours
and then it goes on another 6 hours so I kind of like the scale of the 
psilocybin trip which is 4 to 6 hours and I could see doing this on my birthday 
every year, as like a really good way to 
take stock of where you are in life and set your intentions for the future. ALISON: You talk about 
microdosing in the book, and I can see that that might be 
a great application, because part of the challenge is dosing, and how long you're involved 
in that experience, that could be amazing for people 
as they're aging, or people who have dementia, does that appeal to you, 
or do you think that's a trend, that's part of us getting engaged 
with psychedelics again. MICHAEL: I think the jury's out, I didn't do a lot on microdosing 
in the book, for the reason that I really 
stuck pretty close to the science and the research, and everything is really rooted in that, and frankly, there's no good research
on microdosing yet. There's a lot of anecdotal work, 
there's a psychologist who's collected thousands of reports from people 
who are following a microdosing regime. You take like a tenth of the normal 
dose of LSD or psilocybin, you take it once every 3 or 4 days, and they keep journals for him, 
and they report improvements in mood, and productivity, and creativity, but that could all be placebo effect, there's a very powerful placebo effect with molecules like psychedelics 
because they're so magic in our minds, now there's nothing wrong with
a placebo effect, but I'm curious to know 
in a controlled trial, where some people are getting 
the placebo, and some people are getting
the microdose, are there differences observed then, and we don't know that answer to that yet, so everybody's doing this on faith. ALISON: I wanted to ask you,
if you think one of the dangers of returning to psychedelics, 
which is happening, is the way they were misused 
not by someone like Timothy Leary, but by the government, by the CIA, 
and the mind control experiments, you mention that in the book, 
but you don't go into it, a lot of that information was destroyed, 
but there was a senate investigation, it took place in the period of time 
that you're looking at, that first wave, '53 to '73, and they experimented on people
with drugs, some people knew they were taking drugs,
but many people did not, and we know what the repercussions 
of that have been, and I just wonder, is that a concern
that you have, in this sort of openness, 
how are we going to regulate this, how are we going to legislate it? Do you have any anxiety about these other kinds of use
of psychedelic drugs to control. Because one of the other elements of it 
is the susceptibility that you're under... MICHAEL: Suggestibility, yeah. Well you have the case of Charles Manson who supposedly used LSD in his cult. ALISON: Right, but the CIA was 
working with major universities, institutions, prisons, hospitals. MICHAEL: The CIA funded a lot 
of the research, the researchers didn't realize
who was funding them, but there were these various 
front foundations, that paid for a lot of LSD research
in the 50's. The whole time that there was this very well-intentioned therapeutic research
going on through the 50's and 60's, the CIA had its own program, and they were trying to figure out, basically cause the Nazis 
had used mescaline, and they thought the Russians were
also using some psychedelics, which may have been true, that there was an arms race 
around psychedelics basically, and they were trying to figure out
what might they be good for, how could we weaponize them
in effect? And they went through a series 
of different ideas, one was that it was a truth serum, you would give LSD or psilocybin 
to somebody, and they would not be able to lie to you, but of course, they said 
all sorts of crazy stuff, it was not good, didn't work, And then they thought you could
sneak it to world leaders and get them to do foolish things, 
ala the exploding cigar for Castro, and I don't think that worked very well. ALISON: I think people still
have that fantasy, though. MICHAEL: Yes. But the crazy thing
could include pressing the button, the nuclear button, so I don't understand 
why that was a good strategy, and then, mind control, 
that was another one, that you could get people to do things by suggesting things to them. They did some really, 
just morally abhorrent things, including dosing people 
without their permission. They set up a brothel in both 
New York and San Francisco where they would dose the Johns
and watch what happened, so it was really awful research, and when that came out in the early 70's and there was the Church hearings 
and some other hearings around it, that did contribute to the backlash. So, I think a really important question 
about psychedelics, is the kind of cultural veilance they have. There was a study done in England, that showed that nature 
connectedness rises, my experience is not unusual, 
that that goes up. Tolerance for authoritarianism 
goes down, and the researchers there,
including one I profile in the book, Robin Carhart-Harris, who thinks they have a basically positive
social or political import. But I think that has a lot to do with 
the people who are giving them to people, and the kinds of people they're
giving them to. I think that it's potential they could
be used in a negative way, I think that potential is there. ALISON: Did you come across in the 
people you were meeting, and the research you were doing, 
anyone who'd been involved? MICHAEL: No, those people aren't
around or they're really undercover and the problem is we only know 
a little bit about it, we assume all the experiments failed
for example, but maybe one of them didn't. The other was to put it in 
the water supply for enemy countries and disable the population using LSD. ALISON: It's just fascinating because there's so much at least
in popular culture that seems to still be trying
to work out that aspect of our history
of psychedelics, movies like Jacob's Ladder, 
or that new series Homecoming. MICHAEL: Or Errol Morris did 
a great series, called Wormwood. It seemed to be about the CIA experiments it was about a guy who was given
LSD by the CIA without his permission supposedly went crazy, and jumped out of a window in New York
3 days later. But Morris kind of suggests 
that was a cover story for something much darker. It's a great film, it's on Netflix 
if you haven't seen it. ALISON: So, I was curious about your decision to try 
different psychedelics to me is of a piece with your work, 
which is very drawn to the experiential. You've hunted and gathered, 
and designed and built houses, you've done all kinds of things, you've followed in the footprints
of some of the people you write about, living or not, and why is that important 
to your writing and to you? MICHAEL: I do like finding 
a way to use the first person I'm not a confessional writer, 
this is my most personal book by far, but I find it's a really interesting tool to find a productive way to put yourself 
in the story and a place to stand that gives you 
a perspective that nobody else has. I realized much later
that I had gotten this from a writer that I had read 
when I was a teenager George Plimpton who was a sports writer and he wrote a book I just loved, 
when I was like 13 called Paper Lion. And he was the editor of the Paris Review and her wrote for Sports Illustrated, and he wanted essentially to revive
sports writing which had fallen into a very,
you know, the cigar chomping guy on the sidline, who's very cynical, 
has been there, seen it all, and he got the idea of getting 
on the field and he somehow persuaded the Detroit Lions to let him go through summer training camp and then start as a quarterback in 
one of their exhibition games, very dangerous. More dangerous now, than in the 60's. And he wrote about this with incredible
vividness and humor, and, you could get a quarterback 
to write a book, but of course, he's done it so many times,
he doesn't see it freshly anymore, and there is that wonder that comes 
from doing something for the first time that you never get again, and so even though there are so many more
experienced psychonauts than me, I knew that having done LSD for 
the first time, with the intention of writing about it, would give me a perspective
that would be valuable to write about plus, I'm curious, 
in all these things, I'm curious to know, 
what is it like to do this? What is it like to build? What is it like to own a cow that you're 
following through the meat system? And it gives you comic possibilities too, 
and I think humor is really important no matter what you're writing about. Since you're a fish out of water, you're essentially creating 
a fish out of water, or man from Mars kind of narrative, and there's often comic possibility, but there's also a quality of wonder
you can capture, that you can't get any other way. I had amazing interviews, people that use psychedelics, 
some of them are very eloquent about what happened to them, it's such a transformative experience
for them, and such an indelible experience, 
they remember everything about it, it's not like a dream at all. And I had these great interviews, 
but the more I talked to people, I was like, I wonder how this would 
be for me? And I became, for various 
personal reasons, where I was in my life, the fact that 
I was doing this work, when my dad had a cancer diagnosis
and was dying. And I was interviewing cancer patients who had an experience
that removed their fear, and that completely reset their attitude
toward their mortality, and allowed them to talk about death 
in a way so few of us can, with just openness and candor. And it was a conversation I really 
couldn't have with my father, who processed it very internally, and I still don't know exactly how
he processed it, he died a year ago in January,
yesterday was his birthday, so I really wanted to see what 
this was about, I was just burning with curiosity. But I knew as a literary matter, that this would be good for the book, and that it's what my readers expect 
me to do, so I did it for you. (Laughter) ALISON: One of the things that I like
about you too, in your writing, so you talk about the psychonauts, 
but there's the micoevangelists, like the irrationally exuberant people 
that you referenced before, it's a theme in your work, that you're a little suspicious 
of those people, who have drunk the Kool-Aid, 
rightly so. MICHAEL: But I love them too. ALISON: You love them too, like Paul is a great character 
in this book, well worth reading just for the 
scenes with him, but I was thinking this goes back to, we were talking backstage about how
many of the ideas that are coming to fruition in this book
were ones you were thinking about when you wrote Botany of Desire. What's his name? William Ellery Jones, 
the Johnny Appleseed, and you're a little merciless 
with him, he's just got this story 
of Johnny Appleseed and he's going to stick with it. But I'm curious, 
because you have a healthy sense of skepticism, 
and I think some of that has to do with evolving into a journalist, but where else does it come from? I mean is, is it something about 
your childhood? MICHAEL: Yeah, that's interesting, you know, I do have skepticism, and I think that's been really important
on a subject like this, where so many readers have 
skepticism too, and that most of the books 
written on psychedelics are written by people who 
drank the Kool-Aid a long time ago and are inside the community 
and assume their readers know the territory, 
and everybody is on the same page and I just don't write that way. I like to write, this is in a way
a subculture book, and I like coming in from outside
and having my doubts, and I think that becomes a proxy
for the reader, and makes it easier for 
the reader to go into it, so it serves a rhetorical purpose too. But I really did have my doubts, I was too afraid to use these drugs 
in the 60's, I really didn't do it, 
I just didn't think I was sturdy enough I'd read all the scare stories of 
people jumping off of buildings, and staring at the sun 
until they go blind, and I came of age once those stories
were in the culture, and so I was a really reluctant psychonaut and before every one of my experiences
I had a sleepless night of anxiety of like ping ponging back and forth
of like, are you crazy? You're 60 years old,
you're gonna go up on that mountain with that guy you just met, 
he's completely off the grid, and you're gonna do this drug, and like what if you have a heart attack
is he gonna call 911? And then this other voice saying but aren't you curious 
what you might learn? And you've got a book to write. And it would go back and forth like that fortunately the other voice won and I came to recognize the first voice
as my ego, which our egos command 
our rational faculties and this is an enormous threat to the ego and my ego knew that it was 
about to come under assault, and so was defending itself, so the reluctance was a real thing,
but I think it served a purpose in helping readers get comfortable
with a very uncomfortable subject. ALISON: You say there are other ways 
to achieve this dissolution of the ego meditation, maybe wild dancing, fasting, 
praying, there's all kind of routes, 
certain breathwork, We have a question from a woman 
named Jen who asks, "Do you escape your ego when writing?" MICHAEL: Intermittently. 
there's an experience called flow, by a sociologist with a very long name
that nobody can pronounce some of you know who he is. And flow is that quality of becoming so 
immersed in a task that all self consciousness vanishes
and you are just so present to it and my guess is, someday if we can 
put those people in an FMRI scanner we'll see that something similar 
is going on, that they are quieting the same
network in the brain, the default mode network, 
which appears to be implicated in psychedelic experiences,
and also meditation, so one of the really interesting
neuro scientific findings was when they first started putting people in MRI machines and then giving
them a shot of LSD or psilocybin and imagine that, right? 
These volunteers deserve our thanks. I mean going in an MRI machine 
stone cold sober is terrifying, and they took pictures of their brains
and they were very surprised to find that instead of activating everything, 
as they thought was happening, it deactivated something called 
the default mode network which is closely associated with 
self reflection and time travel and theory of mind, all those things having to to do 
with the construction of self went quiet. And the degree to which it went quiet
correlated precisely with the degree to which people reported 
my ego was obliterated or dissolved. And then they did the same thing
with meditators, different lab, totally 
different protocols, and they put in people who had
10,000 hours of meditation and the same brain networks were quieted which is really interesting considering 
the experience is different but it has in common that loss 
of a sense of ego. ALISON: But you don't think we're
going to be having a national conversation as you said in the op-ed, 
about meditation, right. MICHAEL: In a way we have, in a quiet way without frightening anybody, it's very interesting how far mindfulness 
has come, that's kind of the neutral descriptor
for Buddhist meditation and it's now covered by insurance, mindfulness programs 
if you have high blood pressure. So, the culture has embraced that, 
but it's also interesting to learn that the roots of mindfulness and Buddhism 
in America are psychedelics that all the important American Buddhists the people that brought Buddhism 
to America started out on LSD and psilocybin. I've interviewed a bunch of them, 
and they're pretty candid about it now, there was a little discomfort 
for a few years in talking about it because there's a prohibition 
on intoxicants. But they had this altered form 
of consciousness, this experience of ego loss, and they wanted to figure out 
how to bring that kind of conciousness into every day life, 
cause you can't trip every day, how do you make it a practice? Well meditation is a way to 
make it a practice. And indeed the psychedelic experience
is very helpful to meditation in that is gives you the kind of 
consciousness you're striving for and I talked to one meditation researcher
who did thos FMRIs of meditators and he say he could imagine a day
when we could us psychedelics to kickstart a meditation practice. Because people don't know where they're 
supposed to go, the destination is so fuzzy, and it's not
after you've had psychedelic experience. And I found, in my own case, I became a much more faithful,
and effective meditator after my psychedelic experiences. So I think it's a very logical progression and a healthy progression, 
cause I don't think you want to do these drugs on a weekly basis
like your friend did growing up. ALISON: I wanted to ask you, 
when you talk about America so the American story of psychedelics
involves other cultures and other specific rituals and practices. And there's a great and disturbing story
in the book about R. Gordon Wasson, 
who writes an article in the 50's for Life Magazine, Henry Luce who was the founder of 
Time and Life is a big proponent of LSD so he goes to southern Mexico
to the Mazatec and he meets this woman Maria Sabina, and she takes him 
through the ritual, and then he takes that back, 
writes about it, and then writes a book and kind of exposes where 
this is happening so then everybody goes there
and that culture was trashed. And even Timothy Leary, his first psychedelic experience
was going to Mexico. MICHAEL: Yeah, on psilocybin. ALISON: Yeah, and we think now
of Mexico, talking about the drug war, as the center of the drug war, 
but it's been this well in multiple ways for Americans to tap 
into different rituals different cultures, and different drugs, do you think we have to have a reckoning
with that history, that you write about as we move
towards a cultural acceptance? MICHAEL: There's the cultural 
appropriation that goes on too, without question, and that happened with psilocybin there were these mushroom cults, we call a religion a cult
when we don't like it but when the Conquistadors came to Mexico they found that the native population
was using psilocybin in their rituals, and there are these wonderful accounts
of what it looked like, and they called psicolcybin 
a flesh of the gods, so it was a sacrament and that of course is what 
the Christian sacrament is bread is the flesh of God. And that was very threatening to 
the Catholic church because frankly the psilocybin sacrament
worked a lot better, You didn't need faith, you really saw god,
you met him, or it. So they crushed this brutally. They tortured people 
who were using mushrooms. And it went underground, and survived 
for 500 years underground with no one being aware of it in Mexico and Gordon Wasson who was this banker 
from New York and amateur mycologist re-discovered it and had the first trip. But what happened in its wake, 
is you had a generation of seekers beat generation people, hippies, going to Juatla De Jimenez, this town
where she was, and commercializing it, and the people of the town turned on 
Maria Sabina, and they burned down her house for having done this, for having
ruined their town. We're seeing a little of that with
ayahuasca now too, and so that's a concern, it's interesting that the Indigenous
Americans who use peyote have done a much better job of
defending their cultural use against Westerners, in that they don't
allow many people to participate, they don't sell peyote to anybody outside they're not going around organizing
circles in Brooklyn for peyote, the way there are certain shamans
doing that from South America. So, I think that the indigenous
traditional uses of these drugs have a lot to teach us about 
how to use them well. ALISON: Or even what science,
or spirituality, or knowledge is, right? MICHAEL: Yeah, so it seems to me
there's a way to use that knowledge without abusing it, so people do study that example and when we were talking earlier about designing this 
container for psilocybin or LSD, we should look at those models, 
because there's a lot of wisdom there, there's many more years of 
trial and error. These drugs burst on our culture
in the 50's and 60's and they didn't come with 
an instruction manual, we didn't know what to do, 
there was a lot of reckless use, and experimentation, putting them in punch bowls, 
the acid test ideas, that's kinda crazy. So what do you learn from traditional use? Well, you seldom do it alone, 
only the shaman ever takes the drugs alone there's always a shaman or and elder
or a healer involved, someone who knows the territory
to guide people, it's always done on a very special 
occasion with a very clear intention it's approached with real reverence
it's not casual in any way. So, I think there are things 
we can learn from those examples but I also think we should 
preserve those examples and helping the Indigenous Americans
using peyote, preserving their access to peyote
which is running out, there's a real shortage for them, and those conservation efforts
I think are really important, and wherever else we find these
traditional uses, I think we need to protect them. ALISON: Reading this book
the history of psychedelics is a history of knowledge,
how knowledge is produced, how it can be suppressed, 
how it comes back, it's fascinating on that level, and one of the things that's 
really fascinating about psychedelics is the way it kind of breaks down even as we're trying to keep it 
in multiple containers, to regulate it or control it, it really is about breaking down and you said it really
kind of takes away that binary thinking
that we're all trapped in. And one of the binaries
you were interested in going into this project was between
science and spirituality, and whether they could cross pollinate and have something to say to one another do you feel that that desire you have
is happening as we move in to that broader conversation
about psychedelics? MICHAEL: Yeah, I think that's 
one of the most interesting things is that it is bringing 
science and spirituality in contact with one another,
in a way I didn't think was possible I thought these were totally
incomensurate vocabularies for understanding reality and the very first paper that I describe, that really launches 
this psychedelic renaissance, it was this paper that came out of
Johns Hopkins lab in 2006 which I encourage you to read, 
it's a wonderful paper, the title was like "Psilocybin Can Occasion Mystical Like
Experiences in Healthy Normal People." Really? A science paper about 
mystical experience, how curious, but it turns out, and mystical experience
is defined, beginning with William James as involving ego dissolution, 
transcendence of space and time, sense of well being or ecstasy, 
and a couple other qualities, it appears we're wired for this experience and that's interesting
as a scientific matter. Why would we be? Why would it be adaptive
that our brains can go to this place, this form of consciousness? Because we can achieve it spontaneously,
very religious people achieve it, people doing other things, 
like meditation achieve it, and that's a very curious fact about us. And there's a history of
mystical experiences it goes back thousands of years, 
and ditto the near death experience, which has a lot of commonalities,
maybe that has a chemistry too. In the next few years, we're going to learn some 
really incredible things about the mind, about spiritual experience, about the ego, that we didn't know. And it's gonna be because of these drugs,
and the probe they give us into the brain, science has been stymied trying 
to make sense of consciousness, It does not have tools to really
reach consciousness, the subjective experience of being 
who you are, which is more real to us than 
just about anything. Weirdly enough, science has
no access to that, except our reports of what it feels like, 
or what blue looks like to us, or what love feels like to us. One of the things that psychedelics do
is by disturbing consciousness radically, as they do, they help
force it to reveal some of its secrets and I think there is a rush
of really smart scientists who've gotten interested in psychedelics and we haven't heard from them yet. There's this first generation of very 
courageous scientists who risked their career to do this research, 
most of which is clinical, but now you're going to see 
neoroscientists doing basic science working with psychedelics to see what secrets of consciousness
can be teased out. And I'm really excited by that, 
and I think in the next 5 to 10 years we're gonna know a lot more 
than we do now. ALISON: Well, I hope you'll continue to be our guide, and statesman
on that journey. Thank you so much Michael Pollan, 
it was such a pleasure. MICHAEL: Thank you, 
thanks for your questions. (Applause)

serious active fertile period of research looking at psychedelic compounds such as LSD and psilocybin as a treatment for alcoholism for depression for anxiety for the the what's called existential distress of people with a cancer diagnosis and this work had been going on from 1950 well into the 60s and it had produced some some remarkable successes and manoah's this LSD and psilocybin looked === VIDEO: Asx_qbgYK90 (https://youtu.be/Asx_qbgYK90) ===

when journalist Michael pollen set out to write about the effect of psychedelic drugs on conditions such as depression addiction and anxiety his intention was not to write what is likely his most personal book ever but when he began to discover how these substances have been improving the lives of the mentally ill but also of healthy people dealing with the challenges of everyday life he decided to explore the landscape of the mind for himself in the first person as well as the Third how to change your mind captures pollen's exploration of various Altered States Of Consciousness along with the Deep look into the latest on brain science and the flourishing underground community of psychedelic therapists a unique and elegant blend of memoir science research history and Medicine how to change your mind is a fascinating Deep dive into the exciting and startling New Frontier in our understanding of the mind and human consciousness pollen's writing shows us how in a world that offers us everything from grief to trauma to Beauty to peace we can do our best to find meaning and joy in our lives about the author Michael pollen is a professor of Journalism at the University of California at Berkeley a former executive editor for Harpers and a contributing writer for the New York Times he is also the author of several books including second na nature a Gardener's education a place of my own the education of an amateur Builder the Botany of desire which received the bord original voices award for the best non-fiction work of 2001 the omnivores dilemma in defense of food an eater Manifesto and food rules an eater manual a Renaissance was triggered in 2006 a few two separate events in and around 2006 triggered what pollen characterizes as a Renaissance first was the Centennial Celebration of Albert Hoffman author of LSD my problem child Hoffman in describing one of his first trips wrote my ego was suspended somewhere in space and I saw my body lying dead on the sofa the second event was the unanimous decision of the US Supreme Court that the Uno de vegetal udv or a union of the plants a religious sect that uses iasa as it Sacrament was allowed to import the drink to the United States even though it contains a schedule one substance dimethyl tryptamine DMT udv is a Christian spiritist sect founded in 1961 in Brazil by Jose De Gabriel dasta the Supreme Court's ruling arose from the 1993 religious freedom restoration act which provides that only if the government has a compelling interest can it interfere with one's practice of religion also around this time Roland Griffith published siloc ibin can occasion mystical type experiences having substantial and sustained personal meaning and spiritual significance this was maybe the first rigorously designed doubleblind Placebo controlled clinical study in several decades if not ever to analyze the py ological effect of a psychedelic Griffith recruited Bill Richards a master clinician psychologist to help guide psychedelic trips for participants in the research Richards Bel psychedelics have powers to unleash truths in our lives that we cannot put into words he wrote you have to imagine a caveman transported into the middle of Manhattan he sees buses cell phones skyscrapers airplanes then zap him back into his cave what does he say about his experience it was big it was impressive it was loud he doesn't have the vocabulary for skyscraper elevator cell phone maybe he has an intuitive sense there was some sort of significance or order to the scene but there are words we need that don't yet exists we've got five crayons when he needs 50,000 different shades pollen's experience with magic mushrooms pollen writes that he had become increasingly curious to explore the natural history of magic mushrooms and their strange Powers he wanted to know where they grew and how and why they evolve the ability to produce a chemical compound so closely similar to the neurotransmitter serotonin that the compound is able to temporarily take over the mamalian brain when pollen tripped on magic mushrooms for the first time he saw himself spread out across a wide outdoor landscape like paint he writes that under the influences of acid magic mushrooms and DMT from the Venom of a toad he experienced his sense of self dissolving like butter blowing away like a leaf in the wind and then exploding pollen encountered these Sensations while using all three drugs he tried sometimes more gently and other times more violently he says who was this ey that was able to take in the scene of its own dissolution good question it wasn't me exactly pollen notes that many scientists believe that the chemicals in magic mushrooms and other psychedelic drugs function by essentially rebooting the brain if the brain is stuck in certain narrow ruts of thinking these ruts that lock us into particular patterns of thought and behavior are dissolved and temporarily suspended in a way that allows us to break the patterns our normal defenses are suspended for a period of time and this creates an opening where we can reexamine ourselves and get some perspective on our habitual ways of thinking and doing pollen's LSD trip before pollen experimented with LSD Fritz pollen's LSD guide asked pollen to do a session of holotropic breath work the instructions were straightforward breathe deeply and rapidly while exhaling as strongly as you can pollen quickly went under and was out for over an hour during which time he writes that he felt out of his body afterwards he felt radiant and triumphant his wife later found him unusually chatty and available no longer rushing off to do the next and then the next thing psychedelics in Psychotherapy the the idea that psychedelic drugs could improve your mental health may sound absurd to you after all hallucinogenic drugs have often been blamed for bad trips that have resulted in stays in the psych ward these drugs according to popular culture can literally make you crazy but according to pollen researchers at institutions like New York University John's Hopkins UCLA and Imperial College in London are discovering that when administered in a controlled supportive therapeutic setting psychedelics can actually treat your mental health problems they're also finding that psychedelics may have incredible things to teach us about how the mind works and also why it sometimes fails to work properly and compared to other psychoactive compounds psychedelics are widely regarded as safe and non-addictive it is basically impossible to overdose on a psychedelic rats in a cage presented with a lever to administer drugs like cocaine and heroin will press the lever repeatedly until they die with LSD they press the lever only once of course pollen notes bad trips do sometimes happen especially when psychedelics are used carelessly those who are at risk for schizophrenia may experience psychotic breaks on psychedelics and it is not unheard of for people to do foolish things under the influence that can get them hurt but the extreme claims about the dangers of LS that it scrambles chromosomes or compels users to stare at the sun until blind were discredited a long time ago says pollen so what does the new research tell us a recent pilot study at John's Hopkins examined the potential of psilocybin to help people quit smoking the study was Tiny and not randomized each of the 15 participants received two or three doses of psilocybin and were aware of that fact following the standard protocol in recent psychedelic therapy participants laid down on a couch in a cozily decorated room they wore ey Shades and listened to calming playlists two therapists a man and a woman were present the whole time typically saying very little but available to provide Comfort if necessary the results of the study were amazing six months after their session 80% of the volunteers had quit smoking one year after their session 67% had still abstained this is says pollen a better rate of success than the best treatment now available according to several participants The Experience gave them a new more encompassing perspective on their smoking habit it made smoking seem unimportant and foolish maybe the most important new evidence for the therapeutic value of psychedelics came from trials conducted at John's Hopkins and NYU and published in the Journal of psychopharmacology in 2016 in these trials a single high dose of psilocybin was administered to cancer patients struggling with depression anxiety and the fear of death or recurrence 80 volunteers embarked on a psychic Journey that in many cases brought them face to face with their cancer Their Fear and death a woman with ovarian cancer said I saw my fear Lo located under my rib cage it wasn't my tumor it was this black mass she screamed out loud get the F out and just like that her fear was gone and had not returned years later pollen reports that 80% of the Hopkins cancer patients who received psilocybin showed clinically significant reductions in standard measures of anxiety and depression this effect endured for at least six months after their session the results at NYU were similar very few if any psychiatric interventions for anxiety and depression have provided results like these so how does psychedelic Psychotherapy work when researchers at Imperial College imaged the brains of people on psilocybin they saw that the chemical which they assumed would boost brain activity actually reduced it in a specific area the default mode Network the default mode network is a brain Network involved in a range of metacognitive processes these processes include self-reflection mental time travel the ability to imagine mental States in others and the creation of narratives about ourselves the default mode network is most active when our minds are least engaged in a task that is default mode this is where our minds go when they wander or ruminate the Imperial researchers learned that when volunteers reported an experience of ego dissolution the Imaging of their brains showed a swift drop in activity in the default mode Network which suggests that this network may be the seat of the ego when our boundaries fade or disappear under the influence of psychedelics our ego defenses relax this allows unconscious material and emotions to enter our awareness it also allows us to feel feel less separate and more connected to other people to Nature or to the universe and indeed A Renewed sense of connection is exactly what Volunteers in the various trials for addiction depression and cancer anxiety trials have all reported the value of these experiences is not limited to those who suffer from mental illness or those who face serious illness according to pollen there are powerful implications for achieving betterment of any healthy person we all at some Point get stuck in destructive thought patterns we could all benefit from the mental reboot that psychedelics can deliver paulen writes that one of the lessons of this new research is that even Garden variety unhappiness may come in part from being under the harsh control of the ego that separates us from our emotions from other people and from nature big picture recap our perception of psychedelic drugs has changed people including scientists and medical and mental health professionals are beginning to understand that not all drugs are dangerous in fact some drugs can be very helpful in the summer of 2006 John's Hopkins University School of Medicine neuroscientist Roland Griffiths published an article stating that psychedelic drugs could provide meaningful experiences that contribute to both spiritual and personal development this was based on his scientifically rigorous and Placebo controlled study focusing primarily on psilocybin the substance found in hallucinogenic mushrooms Mexican Indian tribes have been using mushrooms containing psilocybin as a healing agent for centuries it turns out that during a hallucinatory experience the brain is more interconnected memory emotion and visual information are all better able to interact amazingly recent Research indicates that psychedelics could be more effective than Pharmaceuticals in treating depression it has also been shown effective at treating anxiety and addiction this book was dense and difficult to summarize since so much of it was based on anecdote and the author's complicated firsthand experiences with psychedelic drugs in how to change your mind Michael pollen who is perhaps best known for his books related to food shifts his Focus to other uses of plants in this compelling history of psychedelics he examines how decades ago psychedelics first came on the radar as a possible treatment for various mental health conditions but soon fell out of favor because of misperceptions about their dangers now their ability to treat addiction and depression has reignited the scientific community's interest and recent studies show amazing results pollen's exploration of the subject is nuanced and sophisticated he doesn't hold anything back participating in the EXP experiences himself this makes for a very enjoyable and eye-opening read he is an excellent writer and is able to distill very complicated topics down to language that is elegant but simple I definitely recommend this book for fans of the work of pollen and also anyone interested in scientific journalism and the study of the Mind
