[APPLAUSE] MICHAEL POLLAN:
Thank you, David. Thanks very much. Can everybody hear
me in the back? So I get to talk about
my trips, but you don't? It doesn't seem fair. So I'm just talking
for about 10 minutes and just tell you a little bit
about what this book's about, and then I'd love to
take your questions and let your interest
dictate the path that we take this afternoon. First, thanks. Thank you very much for coming. This book is a real
departure for me. Those of you who know me as
someone who writes about food might think it's a
little weird that I'm turning to psychedelics,
but in my head there is some continuity,
as well as change. And the continuity is that
I see my writing about food as part of a larger interest
in writing about nature, and the way we eat affects
nature dramatically-- more than anything else
we do, actually, in terms of changing the
landscape of the planet and the atmosphere. And I mean, it's your
most powerful engagement with the natural world, and
I've always been interested in that engagement. And we use nature and plants,
especially, for many things. Food is an obvious one. Beauty is another, but
something we almost all use plants for that I've always
been kind of curious about is to change consciousness. Most of us today
probably used a plant to change the
contents of your mind, or at least the feeling
tone in your mind, whether it was coffee, or tea,
or a cigarette, or cannabis, or whatever it is. I mean, this is something we
do, and every culture on earth does it with the one exception
that proves the rule. The Inuit do not
have any plant drugs, but it's only because none
of them grow where they live. As soon as they
go somewhere else, they get with the program. So it's been a long
standing interest, and to me, it's all
part of that story about this
fascinating engagement we have with other species
that defines us and reveals a lot about us. And I wrote a little bit about
cannabis and botany of desire, and I wrote a long piece about
growing opium in my garden back in the '90s. And I've written about
medical marijuana, so I had this interest. And then along comes
this new research, which I began reading about. In 2010, I read about a
really interesting odd study where researchers at
NYU and Johns Hopkins were giving psilocybin--
this is the ingredient in magic mushrooms. They were synthesizing it-- to people who had cancer
diagnoses, people very sick, many of them terminal, in the
hopes that they would have what they call the
mystical experience, a powerful spiritual
experience that would change their
attitude toward their death and help them essentially
to die with more peace. These were all patients
who were struggling with depression, anxiety,
and fear-- profound fear. And the stories-- so I started
writing about that, because it seemed like the last
thing I would want to do is trip when I was facing a
terminal diagnosis-- to lose control like that, and
you would presumably have a very dark experience. So I started interviewing
these people for a piece I did in "The New
Yorker" in 2015, and just to give you one example
to give you the flavor of it, I remember talking
to this woman. She was a figure
skating instructor in New York in Manhattan. She was about 60. She was not a psychonaut. She had never used
psychedelics before, and she had ovarian cancer. And her cancer had been
treated successfully, but she was paralyzed by
the fear of recurrence. She just couldn't function
because she thought any day now this could be back. So she entered into
this trial at NYU and had a high dose
psilocybin experience. And I should explain how this is
done, because the image you may have in your head of
taking psychedelics, taking a handful of mushrooms and
going to a concert or something, this is not how it is used
in a clinical setting. It's a very
controlled experience. It's guided. So you work with two
guides, a man and a woman. They prepare you over a
course of several sessions as to what to expect. So they'll talk to you what to
do if the experience becomes frightening, which it often
does, and how to navigate that. They call it the
flight instructions, and their main advice is, if
you see a staircase, go up it. If you see a door, open it. If you see a monster,
don't run away. Just step right
up to it and say, what are you doing in my head? What do you have to teach me? In other words, surrender
to the experience. Trust. They quote John Lennon. Relax the mind and
float downstream. And that's very
important advice, and it's often the difference
between a good trip and a bad trip. A bad trip is essentially
a panic reaction against what's happening in
your head, which is very heavy. I mean, you're experiencing
your ego dissolving, in fact, in front of you. And then they sit with you
for the whole experience, and they give you a
helping hand if you're getting upset or struggling, and
they take you to the bathroom and give you a glass of water. And then afterwards
is a really key moment called the integration session,
where they sit with you. You tell the story of
your trip, and it usually is this narrative. It's almost like this
intra-psychic movie where you go different places. And you try to make sense
of it and apply the lessons to your life. So in the case of this woman--
her name is Dina Bazer-- she had this trip, and there are
many elements I won't go into. It was like six hours. And she went into her
body imaginatively, and she saw this black
mass under her rib cage, and that was her fear. It wasn't her cancer, because
that wasn't in the right place, but she saw what she
realized was her fear. And she screamed at her fear. And you have to see this woman. She's a very timid, small
woman, and she said, get the fuck out of my body. And it did. It disappeared. The black mass was gone. And afterwards, she said, I
realized during that experience that I can't control my cancer,
but I can control my fear, and that distinction
meant everything to her. And one of the hallmarks of
the psychedelic experience is that whatever insights
you have during it have a force the likes of which
you've never experienced. These are not just
opinions or insights. They're revealed truths. This is something
William James a long time ago writing about the
mystical experience called the noetic sense. It's a very uncanny
thing, and it's what allows people to
change that, if they think-- say they're trying
to quit smoking and they have some epiphany
that, gee, my breath is really precious. I should not ruin it by smoking. Suddenly that will be so
strongly held as a belief that they can
actually stop smoking. So anyway, so that's
the kind of experience that I was learning about,
and I became fascinated to understand the
neuroscience behind it, what's going on in the
brain when people are taking one of these trips. And we now know some
interesting things about that. If you're interested,
I can talk about that and also the history of it. What happened to psychedelics
in the '60s that they became so stigmatized
that research stopped? There had been a very
productive period of research all through the '50s
that was yielding really promising results in the
treatment of alcoholism, in dealing with
people who were dying, depression, anxiety, obsession. And then after this
moral panic that hits the culture around 1965,
nobody studies it anymore. The funding dries up. Scientists are a little
embarrassed by it, and we had an
unprecedented situation where a promising line of
scientific investigation was stopped. That never happens, and we
last 30, 35 years of research into psychedelics. Thankfully, it's
resuming, and my book is very much about
the renaissance, even though I go back,
and look at the history, and figure out why we had this
panic reaction about them, and try to look at them
in a very matter of fact way as interesting tools-- interesting tools for
understanding the mind and for healing the mind. Now, we haven't proven all this. There's still more
research to be done. We're kind of in
phase two, and there are three phases to
FDA drug approval, but so far the results have
been remarkably encouraging. It is one of the most powerful
psychiatric interventions that the researchers
have ever seen, and we're in a situation
where mental health care is so broken in this country
it's failing to reach half the people who need it. The last major innovation
were the SSRI antidepressants in the late '80s. There really hasn't been
anything since then. And so we need some
new ways of thinking, and along comes this powerful
old but new innovation. And the research is-- it's being privately
funded for the most part. Big pharma is not
interested in this. There's no intellectual
property that you can control, but a lot of donors, many in
the tech community, actually, have stepped up and are
funding this research. And I was just at a fundraiser
where the last $7 million was raised, and so
we will find out. We'll get the real test,
the scientific test of what are these drugs good for. And then there's
the question of, what value might they
have for the rest of us? Because they help people
who have serious problems, but as one researcher
put it to me, they're very important for
the betterment of well people. And I could talk a little
bit about that, too. So as part of this, because
it's sort of my brand to do immersion journalism-- when I wrote about the cattle
industry, I bought a steer. When I wanted to learn about
architecture, I built a house. So of course I
had to experiment, and I found some
guides underground-- and there is a thriving
community of underground guides in America-- who could give
me an experience very much like the ones at
NYU and Hopkins. And they were transformative
experiences, some of them, some of the most meaningful
experiences in my life. And the idea that a
molecule could occasion that is, to me, still
kind of mind blowing. So, though I've given you
lots of leads and things you might want to
follow up on, and I'm happy to talk about
anything, I advise you not to talk about your
own experiences. I want to welcome the
Google micro dosing club to today's event. [LAUGHTER] Anyway, so yeah, question? AUDIENCE: So I don't want
to be the devil's advocate here, but-- MICHAEL POLLAN: Please do. AUDIENCE: --this idea that
we're going to give somebody this drug, they're going to have
this transformative experience, and then it's going to
change them, I understand. That makes sense
to me, but the idea that that change would
necessarily be for the better-- that person coming back from
a trip and decides they're going to quit smoking. That's great, but why doesn't
a person come back from a trip and decide they need
to kill their neighbor? How do be break
the symmetry there? MICHAEL POLLAN: Set and setting. So one of the important
features of psychedelics is how little the experience
is foreordained or dictated by the drug itself. The drug is like an
unspecific mental amplifier, and so your expectation
and intention is very important
to what happens. And one of the things that
happens in the preparation is that the guide is helping
you set an intention, like I want to quit smoking. I want to confront my mortality. And you will usually have
an experience in that realm because that's what you've
been primed to have. Could the drugs
be used for evil? Well, we have the case
of Charles Manson. Manson apparently-- we
don't know all the details, but used LSD on his little posse
of what would become murderers, and the drugs are
incredibly suggestible. And that you could imagine
a charismatic figure using them that way. Now, the CIA sought to
use them that way, too. The CIA had a very active
research program in the '50s. The same time all of
this university work was going on in the
'50s to help people, the CIA was trying to
weaponize LSD and psilocybin. They went through a series
of different paradigms. One was you would use
it as a truth serum. People could tell you
the truth if you gave it to them in interrogation. People say crazy shit. It didn't work. There was no truth in that. [LAUGHTER] And then they thought,
well, we could use it to have foreign leaders. We could dose them, and they
would do embarrassing things. That might work. I don't know. And we don't know how
far they got with it. And then the other
one was my control, that you could use this drug
to control people's minds. As far as we know, that
didn't work either, but we don't know for sure. And we shouldn't put
too much by them. So I think it all
depends on your therapist and how the situation is. People don't generally
go have violent episodes after psychedelics. In general, the
experience tends to be one where you discover that love
is the most important thing in the universe, and this
Hallmark card platitude suddenly becomes
this profundity. And it is a profundity, but
we've heard it so many times that it's banal. There's an interesting
line between the banal and the profound, and
psychedelics definitely works on that line. So I think we should be
mindful of that possibility, but I don't think-- we certainly haven't
seen anything like that. There have been 1,000
dosings of volunteers since this new wave of
research, and there has not been a single adverse event. No psychotic breaks, no
jumping out of windows, but there is no question that
people can do stupid things on psychedelics, as
they can on alcohol and any number of other drugs. So thanks for your question. AUDIENCE: Awesome. Thank you. AUDIENCE: Do you
have any theories of why our brains are
wired to react to things from plants and fungi? And are there any things
from the animal kingdom that do the same thing? MICHAEL POLLAN: Well, animals
do like plant drugs, also. We have many cases of-- I mean, if you've had a
cat, never give them catnip. AUDIENCE: No, I mean like
any venoms that [INAUDIBLE].. MICHAEL POLLAN: Oh, yes. Well, one of those
psychedelics I experimented with is
something called 5-MeO-DMT. This is a pretty
obscure psychedelic. I see a couple of
nods in the crowd. [LAUGHTER] Actually only one-- that is
the smoked venom of the Sonoran desert toad. Somebody figured
that out, right? I mean, what a species, huh? But why are we wired for this is
a really interesting question. So the way the drugs
appear to work in the mind is that they bind to
your serotonin-- one particular serotonin
receptor that's very common in the
cortex, and they start a cascade of effects. That's what neuroscientists
say when they really don't know what's happening for a while. [LAUGHTER] But that that in turn eventually
down regulates something called the default mode network. I'll tell you what
that is because it's very important, actually,
and it explains a lot. So when they first started doing
fMRI imaging of people's brains on psychedelics,
they would basically inject you with psilocybin and
slide you into the MRI machine, and you'd have what could
be a very scary experience. [LAUGHTER] And then they took
these pictures, and they expected to see
lots of extra activity because they're such
fireworks in the experience, but they were very
surprised to find that, in fact, this
particular network went quiet. And this is a network
that's very active when you're not on task. It's in a seesaw relationship
with the attentional networks when you're working or
something like that, but when you're asked to just
lie back in an MRI machine, and they're trying to get
a baseline, this lights up. And they were like, why is that? What is it doing? Well, this appears to be
the part of your brain that is active when
you're mind wandering, when you're ruminating, or
worrying, or reflecting on yourself. It's a center of
self-reflection. It's also involved in
time travel, the ability to think about the
future or the past. And it's involved
in theory of mind. This is the term for
the ability to think about the mental states
of other people-- central to compassion, and
imagination, and things like that. And lastly, it's involved in
something called the narrative self or experiential

you knit together what happens to you at any given
moment with the story of who you are based on who you've
been and who you want to be. And it's kind of
where we sustain that story that our
sense of self depends on. And so this network-- so if the ego has
an address, it's in the default mode network. This is what the research shows. It's really
interesting that this is what gets down regulated
during the experience, and it chimes with
the experience of ego dissolution people report
on a high dose experience. I had an experience
on a guided psilocybin trip where I felt
my sense of self scattered to the wind like
a blizzard of post-its. And then I looked
out, and I don't know. Who's the I who's
looking out at this? I mean, there was a
split in consciousness where I could watch
myself then get spread over the landscape like a
coat of paint out there, and I was fine with it. This other self
was fine with it. And it was this kind of very
imperturbable consciousness that could behold this
thing, and I realized I'm not identical to my ego. And that's a very important
insight to acquire, I think, and very useful. But what was going
on in my brain was the default mode
network was going offline for a period of time. The sense of self dissolves,
and then something else happens, which is other networks in the
brain which normally would only communicate through
this hub start talking directly to each other. And there's a map in the book
of the brain on a placebo and the brain on psilocybin. And one looks like the route
map of American Airlines, and the other looks
like the route map of some little commuter line
that only goes to 10 places. And so new connections form, and
what are those new connections? I mean, that's the
interesting question. Is that, say, your hippocampus
talking to your visual cortex, and so you start
having images of things that you fear or desire? Is it the basis
of hallucination, or is it perhaps the basis of a
new metaphor, or a new insight, or new meme? We don't know yet. That's the next step-- is
to really understand that. So these are temporary
changes in the brain. Eventually your ego
kind of reconsolidates, but having had a glimpse of that
other way to behold experience is very powerful, and in

this amazing experience of ego dissolution. And I realized I'm not
identical to my ego. I can have a little

little less trigger happy. And you can cultivate that. And I said, how? And she said,
through meditation. And meditation is kind
of how you keep alive the psychedelic experience. It makes you a very
good meditator, although I know
you're not supposed to say good or bad
meditator, but being trapped in that language. So that's kind of a real

fascinating, and for me, that chapter of the book was

fascinating to learn about. So thanks for your question. AUDIENCE: Thank you. AUDIENCE: Hi. MICHAEL POLLAN: Hi. AUDIENCE: So I have

about what that entails or if there's any research that
hints to it being as strong? MICHAEL POLLAN: I mean,
there's anecdotal research that's really interesting. So one very relevant fact-- at the same time we were
doing this imaging work on psilocybin in

these are people with really long
experience meditation in that they can almost at will
probably quiet the default mode network. There's a famous story about

become a guru basically, and he's still alive. He's in Hawaii. And he was very

spiritual plane on this drug, and that's my territory. And so Ram Dass
apparently gave him 600 micrograms of LSD, which
is a beyond heroic dose. And that's what the guy wanted. He said, give me as
much as you have. [LAUGHTER] And the monk-- or
whatever he is. He's a guru, I guess-- takes
it, and nothing happened. [LAUGHTER] Is this true? I mean, Ram Dass
tells this story and swears that it's true,

achieve the same effect. Psychedelics are
probably a shortcut to becoming a really good
meditator, but my guess is we will find at some point

disciplines that share this sense of
transcending the ego, are doing the same

that's happening. And I'm thinking of

the right kind of music, and breathing exercises. One of the psychedelic trips
I had involved no drugs-- something called
holotropic breathwork. It's based on a yogic breathing
exercise where you essentially hyperventilate. You breathe really fast. You exhale more than you inhale,
and you enter a trance state. It's the most uncanny
thing, and my guess is that hyperventilation is
starving the default mode network of oxygen and
reducing the activity there. So this is all areas
to be explored. We may find other ways to
achieve the same thing, but I think it's all connected. AUDIENCE: Thank you so much. MICHAEL POLLAN: Thank you. AUDIENCE: Hi. MICHAEL POLLAN: Hi. AUDIENCE: One of the

a handful of mushrooms and went to a concert, or we
drank LSD from the punch bowl. And I think that's why some
people really got in trouble. And so the lesson that
we failed to learn was that, yes, these drugs
have been ancient lineage, but look how they were used. Look at what those cultures
learned by trial and error. And now we see it in shamanic

in a group with guidance with someone who wasn't
taking the drugs, and with enormous care. And I think that's
what we lost, and we need to now invent that
container for ourselves. Maybe it's this therapeutic
protocol I'm describing. I'm not sure, but
that's the cultural work that needs to be done. AUDIENCE: Thank you. MICHAEL POLLAN: Sure. AUDIENCE: Hi. I was wondering whether

and one's food, and if he presses the lever,
he gets a dose of cocaine, and they'll press till they die
over and over and over again. You put LSD in that contraption. They'll press it

that they interfere with people's life

panic reactions. But often psychiatrists who
didn't know this territory would misinterpret a panic
reaction as a psychotic break, because it sure looks like that. You're having delusions. You're hearing voices. I mean, to an
average psychiatrist, this would say psychosis, but
often it was a panic attack. I interviewed Andrew Weil,
Dr. Andrew Weil, about this, and he was very interested
in psychedelics in the '60s. He was at Harvard

someone's more fucked up than I am, and they

completely subside. So suggestibility is a very
big part of the experience. AUDIENCE: Thank you very much

Usually, um, when we talk about these massive history-altering events, we picture them happening on this grand, highly visible scale. >> Right, like a treaty being signed or a war ending. >> Exactly. Or, you know, maybe most dramatically, a bomb detonating. Something that just physically shakes the foundations of the world. >> Yeah. But midway through the 20th century, there were actually two completely world-altering bombs dropped on humanity at almost the exact same historical second. >> It's really one of the craziest synchronicities. It is. Because, I mean, one of them was literal, right? The splitting of the uranium atom, unleashing the atomic bomb, which obviously changed the geopolitical and physical landscape of the planet forever. >> The ultimate manifestation of the material sciences. >> Yeah. Terrifying physical power. But the other bomb was completely invisible. It didn't happen in a desert. It happened on a microscopic scale inside the human brain. Because just as physicists were splitting the atom to, you know, conquer the physical world, a chemist synthesized LSD in a quiet little Swiss laboratory. And simultaneously, psilocybin mushrooms, which had been violently suppressed for literally centuries, were suddenly reintroduced to the modern West. It was an explosion, but a complete explosion of consciousness. >> So, our mission for this deep dive is to really unpack this. Because it's wild. You have the ultimate destructive power over the physical world arriving at the exact same historical moment as these molecules that completely dissolve the psychological structures of the self. >> Two extraordinary new energies just loosed upon the world. And from that collision, things would just never be quite the same again. >> Yeah, definitely not. We're looking at a narrative that stretches from um, accidental chemistry to indigenous rituals, and then eventually into the sterile, highly regulated corridors of modern institutions like Johns Hopkins. Right. And to really understand the modern psychedelic renaissance happening right now, we have to go back. We have to look at the forgotten first wave of research from the 1950s and then trace the underground network that kept it alive through all those decades of prohibition. And we also have to step out of the laboratory entirely. Right. >> We need to view this from an evolutionary perspective. Because we have to ask that fundamental biological question. Why does a fungus produce a chemical that perfectly fits into the serotonin receptors of the human brain? Which is just a mind-bending But before we dive into the history, I want you the listener to think about your own mind for a second. Just as you're listening to this. We all rely on these mental habits, right? >> Absolutely. Constantly. >> We're running these deeply ingrained neurological algorithms that just get us through the day. You don't have to relearn how to make your coffee every morning. You have a pattern response for how to talk to your boss or how to navigate highway traffic. Even how to perceive a tree in your front yard. We are highly, highly efficient prediction machines. Exactly. And the core of the source material we are unpacking today is really about exploring what happens when a molecule completely disables those algorithms. What happens when the filters are just chemically removed? It opens a door to what the philosopher William James called uh potential forms of consciousness entirely different. >> Yeah. And to grasp the modern clinical trials that are actually testing those forms of consciousness today, we have to understand how these two molecules even got to the West in the first place. Because it was shockingly almost entirely accidental. The accidental nature of it really cannot be overstated. I mean, let's start with the first molecule, LSD. It was synthesized by a Swiss chemist named Albert Hofmann back in 1938. >> And he wasn't looking to expand his mind, right? >> Not at all. He was working for the pharmaceutical firm Sandoz and he was essentially just looking for a circulatory stimulant. Something that could be used as a blood pressure medication or maybe to stop bleeding. >> Standard pharmaceutical stuff. Exactly. He was tasked with isolating compounds from ergot. And ergot is this parasitic fungus that infects grain, particularly rye, and it has this incredibly fascinating, really dark history of its own. Oh, yeah, the St. Anthony's fire stuff. >> Right. When people eat bread made from ergot-infected grain, it causes a condition historically known as St. Anthony's fire or ergotism. It severely restricts blood flow, which causes gangrene, but it also causes profound madness, convulsions, and hallucinations. >> Which, side note, historians have heavily theorized was the actual root cause of the Salem witch trials. It makes a lot of sense when you look at the weather records. Yeah. You had this particularly damp, cold season in Massachusetts, which is perfect for ergot to grow on the rye supply. And suddenly this entire community just starts experiencing mass hallucinations and convulsions, which, of course, they interpreted as literal demonic possession. But, on the flip side of that really dark history, medieval midwives had also used controlled, tiny amounts of ergot for centuries to induce labor and stop postpartum bleeding. So, Sandoz sees a financial opportunity here. Right. They wanted to find a marketable, stable drug hidden within the complex chemistry of this fungus. So, Hoffman is synthesizing various compounds, and he creates the 25th molecule in the series, lysergic acid diethylamide, LSD 25. And what happened? Did they test it? They did. Sandoz tested it on lab animals, and they noted that the animals became, you know, slightly restless, but they saw absolutely no obvious pharmacological value. So, they shelved it. Just filed away? Filed it away as a failed experiment in a corporate lab, and it sat in the dark for 5 years. 5 years, and then what, 1943? April 1943, World War is raging across Europe, and Hoffman experiences what he later described as a peculiar presentiment. >> that phrase. >> It's so unscientific, right? He basically just liked the chemical structure of LSD 25. He had this strange, unshakable gut feeling that it possessed properties he had missed during those initial animal trials. >> So, he just decides to make more of it based on a hunch. Driven entirely by intuition, he decides to synthesize a fresh batch. Now, Hoffman was famous for his immaculate laboratory procedures. He was a meticulous, highly disciplined chemist. >> Which makes what happens next kind of hilarious. >> It really does. Somehow, during the synthesis, he accidentally absorbs a microscopic amount of the chemical. The prevailing theory is that he maybe rubbed his eye with a contaminated finger. So, he accidentally initiated the world's first acid trip. Literally the first one. He had to stop his work and go home because he suddenly began experiencing this uninterrupted stream of fantastic pictures, extraordinary shapes, and just this kaleidoscopic play of colors. Wow. But, because he's this rigorous scientist, he didn't just write it off as like a weird bout of food poisoning. No, a few days later, he decides to conduct a controlled, intentional experiment on herself. He measured out 0.25 mg, dissolved it in a glass of water, and drank it. >> Okay, wait. 0.25 mg, a quarter of a milligram. >> Which sounds tiny. >> Right. For almost any other pharmaceutical compound, a quarter of a milligram is barely a threshold dose. It's a tiny speck. It might not even trigger a physiological response. And Hoffman was operating on those standard pharmacological assumptions. But, what he didn't know, what literally no one on Earth knew at that moment, was that LSD is one of the most potent psychoactive compounds ever discovered. >> active in micrograms, right? Not milligrams. >> Exactly, micrograms. Millions of a gram. So, his cautious, deliberate dose of 0.25 mg was in reality a massive, overwhelming dose. Oh, boy. And this is the famous bicycle day. This is bicycle day. Because of wartime restrictions on automobiles in Switzerland, Hoffman's assistant had to help him pedal home on a bicycle. And while they're riding, the chemist's entire concept of reality just disintegrates around him. >> I can't even imagine riding a bike through wartime Switzerland while your ego is dissolving. Familiar objects on the streets of Basel turn into these grotesque, threatening forms. He felt completely possessed by a demon. He was convinced he was going permanently insane or that he had fatally poisoned himself. He actually described the sensation of his ego being suspended somewhere in the room, right? Like he was helplessly watching his own dead body lying on the sofa. It is the ultimate nightmare scenario. But the physical reality was telling a completely different story. They managed to call a doctor to the house, and Hoffman is fully expecting this doctor to tell him he's in critical organ failure. >> Right, he thinks he's dying. But the doctor examines him and finds that Hoffman's vital signs, his heart rate, blood pressure, respiration, they are all perfectly normal. The only physical symptom was extremely dilated pupils, I think. >> That's it. The profound terror he was experiencing was entirely psychological. And then as the acute peak of the drug wore off, the horror was replaced by this overwhelming sense of gratitude and wonder. Yeah, afterglow. Yeah, he wrote that when he walked into his garden the following morning, everything sparkled in a fresh light as if the world had been newly created. He called it an ademic afterglow. Like Adam waking up in the Garden of Eden. Exactly. And Hoffman emerged from this harrowing experience convinced that this microscopic molecule held profound potential for psychiatry. He believed it could serve as a model for studying mental illness, specifically schizophrenia. Okay, so that is the first bomb. Synthesized in a sterile laboratory, born from a pharmaceutical quest for blood pressure medication. But the second bomb, psilocybin, that wasn't created in a beaker. No, it It been hiding in plain sight, deeply intertwined with human culture for thousands of years. The indigenous peoples of Mesoamerica had utilized these mushrooms, which the Aztecs revered as teonanacatl, meaning flesh of the gods, as a spiritual sacrament for centuries. >> Until the Spanish conquistadors arrived. >> Right. When the Spanish and the Roman Catholic Church arrived, they brutally suppressed the practice. The Inquisition viewed the ingestion of the mushrooms and the visions they produced as literal devil worship. >> So, to survive, the mushroom cults just retreated deep into the mountains, going entirely underground, hidden from the Western world. And it stayed hidden until a Wall Street banker went on vacation. Which is honestly one of the most bizarre historical plot twists imaginable. It really is. Because the popular narrative usually credits the 1960s counterculture with bringing psychedelics to America. But the guy who actually introduced magic mushrooms to the mainstream was R. Gordon Wasson, a vice president of public relations at J.P. Morgan. A very straight-laced establishment guy. >> Yeah. And the origin of his journey started on his honeymoon in the Catskills back in 1927. His bride, Valentina, was a Russian-born physician. And during a walk, she found some wild mushrooms in the woods and was just ecstatic. She gathered them up to cook for dinner. And Wasson was horrified. Genuinely horrified. He came from this strict Anglo-Saxon background, and he was convinced she was gathering poisonous toadstools. He absolutely refused to eat the meal she prepared because he thought he'd wake up a widower. And that little marital friction over a bowl of wild mushrooms sparked a lifelong obsessive fascination for Wasson. He wanted to understand why human cultures are fundamentally divided into two camps. Mycophilic and mycophobic. Right. Mycophilic, meaning mushroom-loving cultures, like the Russians. And mycophobic, meaning mushroom-fearing cultures, like the English. He and Valentina spent decades researching this cultural divide. >> Which eventually leads him to Mexico. Yes. His obsessive research led him to reports of a surviving mushroom cult in the remote mountains of Oaxaca. So, in 1955, Wasson traveled there and sought out a Mazatec curandera, a healer named Maria Sabina. And he actually participated in the ritual. He did. After building some trust, he persuaded her to let him participate in a secret night time ritual of a lota. Wasson consumed these acrid, earthy-tasting mushrooms in the dark. And he later described experiencing these incredibly vivid visions, resplendent palaces, mythological beasts, geometric patterns. >> He felt like he was seeing the foundational archetypes of reality, right? The Platonic ideas that underlie the physical world. >> Exactly. But Wasson was a PR man at his core. He didn't just keep this mystical experience to himself in a diary. He leveraged his connections and went straight to Life magazine. The biggest publication in the country at the time? In 1957, Life published a massive 15-page spread detailing his experience in the Mexican mountains. And fun fact, it was actually the headline writer at the magazine, not Wasson himself, who coined the term magic mushrooms. >> Oh, really? I didn't know that. >> Yeah. And that issue reached millions of American households. Shortly after, Wasson sent physical specimens of the mushrooms over to Albert Hofmann in Switzerland. The LSD guy. The very same. Hofmann managed to isolate the active biochemical compounds from the mushrooms and named them psilocybin and psilocin. So, by the late 1950s, the Western scientific establishment suddenly has both LSD and synthesized psilocybin at their disposal. >> And the initial reaction of the medical establishment to these molecules is a chapter of history that has been almost entirely erased by the cultural narratives of the '60s. >> Oh, completely. Because people think it was just Timothy Leary in a tie-dye shirt passing it out. Exactly. But Sandoz Pharmaceuticals, who held the patent for LSD, they honestly didn't know what to do with it. It didn't cure a specific physical ailment, and the psychological effects were just too unpredictable for standard pharmaceutical marketing. >> So, they did something that is completely alien to how modern drug development works today. They basically adopted an open-source approach. They crowd-sourced the research. Sandoz offered free vials of LSD to any medical professional, psychiatrist, or researcher who promised to administer it and just write up their clinical observations. >> They literally mailed boxes of acid to anyone with a medical degree and said, "Let us know what this does." And this sparked what researchers now view as a golden age of psychiatric research. Between 1950 and 1965, over 40,000 subjects were tested globally. It resulted in over 1,000 peer-reviewed clinical papers. >> That is a staggering amount of data. Major medical organizations were hosting massive international scientific symposiums dedicated entirely to LSD research. This is the absolute vanguard of the psychiatric establishment. >> were they actually doing with it? How were they treating people? Well, researchers like Humphry Osmond and Abram Hoffer, working out of a hospital in Saskatchewan, Canada, they were doing some of the most groundbreaking work. Initially, they operated under what was called the psychotomimetic model. Meaning mimicking psychosis. >> Right. Because LSD caused vivid hallucinations, distorted thinking, and ego dissolution, psychiatrists hypothesized that the drug essentially simulated the symptoms of schizophrenia. >> So, they thought, "Hey, if we take this, we can understand our schizophrenic patients better." >> Exactly. It was an extraordinary paradigm where the doctor became the patient to understand the pathology from the inside out. >> But, they pretty quickly realized that the psychotomimetic model was fundamentally flawed, right? >> Yeah. They found out the state induced by LSD wasn't just simulating schizophrenia. It possessed profound therapeutic potential in its own right. Hoffer and Osmond found tremendous success using high-dose psychedelic therapy to treat chronic, intractable alcoholism. >> Which is fascinating. They realized that the overwhelming, ego-dissolving nature of the drug could simulate the psychological phenomenon of hitting rock bottom, which is usually what precedes recovery in addicts. >> And didn't Bill Wilson, the founder of Alcoholics Anonymous, participate in these trials? He did. He was so moved by the experience that he actively campaigned to incorporate LSD therapy into the AA program in the 1950s. He argued that the drug could reliably induce the necessary spiritual awakening required to break the cycle of addiction. >> Which, obviously, the AA board was not thrilled about. But the therapeutic results they were getting were staggering, right? Often achieving sobriety rates far beyond traditional talk therapy. >> The data was incredibly promising. But despite this monumental body of evidence, the research was systematically dismantled. >> Because the drugs leaped out of the lab. >> Yes. They jumped out of the controlled clinical laboratory and into the volatile cultural landscape of the 1960s. The psychiatric framework was completely shattered by the counterculture. Right. Figures like Timothy Leary, the Harvard psychologist who became this huge psychedelic evangelist, encouraging the youth to turn on, tune in, drop out. And the molecules became inextricably linked to anti-war protests, anti-establishment rebellion, and this perceived existential threat to traditional American values. And the Nixon administration saw an opportunity. They recognized that they could use drug prohibition as a political weapon to blunt the momentum of the counterculture. It was a highly effective cultural suppression. Because by 1970, the Controlled Substances Act classified LSD and psilocybin as Schedule I drugs, meaning they defined them as having a high potential for abuse and absolutely no accepted medical use. Despite the thousands of clinical papers proving otherwise. >> It's as if we discovered the microscope, used it to map the cellular world for 15 years, and then collectively decided to lock it in a vault and pretend cells don't exist. It's an incredible tragedy for medical science. It created a dark age for psychedelic research that lasted for decades. Funding vanished overnight, university research programs were shuttered, and a generation of brilliant researchers were forced to pack up their desks. So, with the science locked in a vault, how did we get to the current clinical renaissance? Because clearly it's back. Getting out of that dark age is a story of quiet, methodical subversion. It required a network of dedicated outsiders who operated below the radar. They understood that reviving the science required navigating immense bureaucratic and cultural hostility. And one of the most pivotal figures in this underground effort was a guy named Bob Jesse. Yes, and Bob Jesse was not a psychiatrist, nor was he a pharmacologist. He was a computer engineer, a highly successful vice president at Oracle in the 1990s. >> A Silicon Valley executive operating in the hyper-logical world of software development. But he harbored a secret. He did. In his 20s, Jesse had a profoundly transformative experience with LSD. He described taking a significant dose and lying under a ficus tree in an apartment in Baltimore. >> As one does. Right. And during the peak of the experience, he felt his individual ego completely slip away. He entered a state of pure, unbounded awareness. He witnessed this incredible vision of the birth of the physical universe. He called it the Big Bang without the boom. Exactly. He watched the emergence of cosmic dust, the formation of stars, the arrival of biological life, the evolution of language, all the way up the evolutionary ladder to himself lying in that specific room. Wow. He emerged from this experience with a deeply shifted worldview. He started harboring a strong suspicion that consciousness is not merely a byproduct of the brain, but might actually precede the physical universe. And he realized that the traditional scientific materialist worldview had zero framework for processing an experience like that. >> None at all. So, in the late 1990s, he took a leave of absence from Oracle and essentially dedicated his life and his resources to reviving legitimate psychedelic research. He formed a non-profit organization called the Council on Spiritual Practices. >> He basically became a historical archaeologist for psychedelics. He really did. He tracked down the surviving psychedelic elders, the researchers, therapists, and guides from the '50s and '60s who were still alive. Many of them were quietly exchanging information at places like the Esalen Institute in Big Sur. And Jesse had his brilliant strategic mind. He realized that if this science was ever going to return to the mainstream, it could not be associated with counterculture baggage. No tie-dye, no Leary stuff. Exactly. It had to be executed perfectly, conducted at a world-renowned institution, and led by researchers whose reputations were absolutely beyond reproach. >> Which set the stage for the year 2006. Mhm. That year stands as the absolute hinge of history for the modern psychedelic renaissance, because there were three massive pillars established in that single year. The first was a cultural pillar. In Basel, Switzerland, thousands of scientists, psychiatrists, and historians gathered to celebrate the 100th birthday of Albert Hofmann. Right. A huge, highly visible symposium honoring Hofmann and his problem child, LSD. It served as a powerful signal to the global scientific community that the psychedelic research network had survived the dark ages. They were organized and they were ready to return to the light. And then the second pillar established the legal groundwork. This involved a small Christian spiritualist sect known as the UDV, which uses a hallucinogenic tea called Ayahuasca as its central religious sacrament. Right. They were targeted by the Bush administration who attempted to seize their imports, and the case went all the way to the US Supreme Court. >> And what happened? In a unanimous 2006 ruling under the Religious Freedom Restoration Act, the court determined that the federal government could not prohibit this group from using their psychedelic sacrament. >> Unanimous, that's huge. >> It was. Now, it was a narrow ruling specific to religious freedom, but it created a vital legal loophole for cognitive liberty. It proved that the absolute prohibition of psychedelics could actually be legally challenged. >> But the third, and arguably most important pillar, was the publication of a landmark scientific paper by Johns Hopkins University. Orchestrated behind the scenes by Bob Jesse. Right, the Hopkins trio. So you have Jesse, the visionary funder and strategist. Second, you have Bill Richards, a veteran psychotherapist. >> Richards is such a crucial historical bridge. He actually administered the very last legal FDA-approved dose of psilocybin in the United States back in 1977, right before the research doors completely slammed shut. >> He had just spent decades quietly maintaining his clinical protocols, waiting for the political climate to thaw. And the third member was Roland Griffiths. And Griffiths is the most interesting part of this to me. Because he was the ultimate strategic choice to lead the study. He was a rigorous, highly respected, profoundly straight-arrow psychopharmacologist at Hopkins. His entire career had been dedicated not to psychedelics, but to the neurobiology of it. Absolutely. However, by the late 1990s, Griffiths was experiencing a deep professional malaise. He was successfully running drug trials and publishing papers, but he found the work increasingly vacuous. Because of what was going on in his personal life. >> Right. He had recently taken up a rigorous Siddha Yoga meditation practice. And he had begun experiencing profound mystical awakenings. He was encountering states of consciousness, feelings of deep interconnectedness that his strict materialist scientific framework simply could not explain. He was seriously considering abandoning his career to move to an ashram in India. So if you're listening right now, you have to ask, "Why would a hardcore behavioral scientist, a guy who studied the chemical hooks of addiction, risk his pristine academic reputation to study mystical experiences triggered by illegal drugs?" Well, Bob Jesse was introduced to Griffiths and made him a really compelling pitch. Jesse argued that they could use psilocybin to reliably, scientifically trigger the exact same mystical states Griffiths was encountering during his deep meditation. >> That's a great hook. It was. Jesse promised that if Griffiths agreed to lead the study, they would design it with absolute unimpeachable scientific rigor to shield it from criticism. And Griffiths accepts. But securing approval was a bureaucratic mountain right? >> A total nightmare. They had to navigate five separate layers of review spanning the FDA, the DEA, and multiple internal ethics boards at Johns Hopkins. The university administration must have been terrified. >> Oh, they feared that associating Hopkins with 1960s counterculture drugs could jeopardize millions of dollars in federal grant money. But Griffiths' scientific reputation was so unassailable that he managed to push the protocol through the gauntlet. And once they got the green light, the experiment they set up was a radical departure from typical medical trials. Historically, medicine is entirely focused on pathology. Fixing a broken system, curing a disease, alleviating a symptom. Griffiths and Jesse, however, were explicitly looking at the betterment of well people. That is such a fascinating phrase. They recruited healthy volunteers with no history of mental illness or psychedelic use. Their objective was to determine if a high dose of psilocybin could reliably occasion a mystical type experience in a controlled setting and to measure the long-term psychological impact. And the environment they created for this, the Hopkins transformation factory, is just a master class in psychological design because they knew that the set and setting is just as critical as the chemical itself. >> Right. If you put someone in a sterile MRI machine under fluorescent lights wearing a hospital gown and inject them with a powerful psychedelic, you are almost guaranteeing a terrifying experience. >> bad trip. >> So, they designed a room that resembled a comfortable dimly lit den. It featured a plush couch, vaguely spiritual artwork on the walls, and a small Buddha statue on the mantel. And they gave them the psilocybin in a pill, presented in a small chalice to elevate the gravity of the moment. Then they lay down on the couch, put on eye shades, and listen to a curated playlist of classical music through headphones. The eye shades and headphones are essential components. In a recreational setting, people often focus outward on the visual hallucinations, the breathing walls, the colors. By blindfolding the participants, the Hopkins team forced the psychological journey entirely inward. They weren't looking at the external world. They were navigating the deep architecture of their own minds. And throughout this internal journey, they were monitored by two trained therapists, guided by a set of flight instructions developed by Bill Richards. I find the flight instructions so fascinating because they run completely counter to our standard psychological defense mechanisms. >> Absolutely. The core mantra Richards drilled into the volunteers was, "Trust the trajectory. TLO. Trust, let go, be open." Right. Richards explicitly warned them that they might feel sensations of dying or melting or completely dissolving into nothingness. And his instruction was, "If that happens, do not fight for your life. Just let the death happen." Which is terrifying. If a volunteer encountered a terrifying monster or a manifestation of the deepest trauma, the instinct is to run away. Richards instructed them to step directly into the monster's mouth. >> Confront the fear and ask it, "What are you doing in my mind? What can I learn from you?" It requires a monumental leap of faith because it contradicts the foundational human survival instinct. But clinically, resisting the dissolution of the ego is the primary catalyst for a bad trip. When the ego fights to maintain control against the neurochemical tide, it just generates intense anxiety. Complete surrender is the mechanism of passage. Now, to ensure the scientific validity, it was a double-blind trial. In some sessions, they received psilocybin. In others, an active placebo, typically Ritalin. Why Ritalin? Because it induces a noticeable physiological shift, increased heart rate, heightened alertness. It convinced the volunteers they had taken a drug, but it doesn't occasion a psychedelic state. This allowed them to isolate the specific effects of the psilocybin. And the results of this 2006 study just sent shockwaves through the psychiatric community. When the data was unblinded, the findings were totally unprecedented. 70% of the participants rated the psilocybin experience as among the top five most meaningful experiences of their entire lives. Ranking alongside the birth of a first child or the death of a parent. Generated by a single pill in a living room at Johns Hopkins. It fundamentally reorganized their internal hierarchy of meaning. And even more astonishing was the durability of the effect. This wasn't a fleeting euphoria that faded the next day. Follow-up evaluations conducted months, sometimes over a year later, revealed that the volunteers showed a sustained, statistically significant increase in the personality trait of openness. Which usually locks into place by age 30. Right. In established models of psychology, adult personality traits don't change. The idea that a single pharmacological intervention could durably increase an adult's openness to new ideas, their creativity, and their tolerance for opposing viewpoints contradicted decades of accepted dogma. To truly grasp the gravity of this data, we have to look at the subjective experiences of these volunteers. Take Brian Turner, for example. He was a physicist, a man with a military security clearance, hyper-rational. During his session, Turner experienced a profound sensation that his physical body was dissolving starting from his feet and moving upward. He reached a point where he felt only the left side of his jaw remained intact. His logical mind deduced that if this final piece disappeared, he would physically cease to exist. So, panic sets in. But then, he remembered the flight instructions. He consciously decided to stop resisting. He leaned into the feeling of his own death, and the moment he surrendered, the terror instantly evaporated replaced by a euphoric floating sensation. He said he was transported into a massive crystalline cave of icicles. And inside each icicle was a perfectly preserved memory of a person from his past. He spent the rest of the session reviewing every relationship that had constructed his identity. The experience so profoundly reoriented his values that he eventually resigned from his military-industrial job and became a practicing Zen monk. It's just incredible. Then there's Amy Sharney, a clinical herbalist. Her session began with this intense psychological darkness. She hallucinated she was standing on a public stage in the 1800s, and a hostile mob was placing a hangman's noose around her neck. Crushed by an overwhelming sense of guilt and shame, but rather than fighting it, she utilized the protocol. She vocally confessed the feeling of guilt to her guide, Bill Richards. The simple act of naming the fear broke its power. The imagery of the noose pixelated and vanished, and she was instantly launched into a transcendent out-of-body experience. She felt like she was flying across the globe, eventually joining a global drumming circle of indigenous healers. For her, it served as a profound spiritual validation of her life's work. >> And finally, we have Richard Boothby, a philosophy professor who carried this unimaginable burden of grief. He had recently lost his son to suicide. During his session, Boothby felt like he was being plunged into an ecstatic catastrophe of logic. His philosophical mind tried to analyze the experience, but the psilocybin continuously shattered his conceptual frameworks. He felt like he was dying over and over again. But when he finally surrendered, he emerged with a single crystalline insight. And that insight was simply love conquers all. Which, to Boothby the rigorous academic, sounded like a profound cliché. Like something printed on a cheap greeting card. But the drug bypassed the intellectual critique. He didn't just understand the phrase, it was branded into his consciousness as an indisputable fundamental law of the universe. To understand why a cliché suddenly feels like a profound revelation, we have to view these reports through the lens of William James. James identified four specific marks that define a true mystical experience. Let's unpack those. The first mark is ineffability. The experience inherently defies language. The volunteers constantly struggled in post-session interviews saying, "You just had to be there." >> Our vocabulary is built for the 3D physical world. It lacks the architecture to describe the dissolution of time and space. What's the second mark? The noetic quality. This is crucial. It's the overwhelming feeling that what you are experiencing is an authoritative revealed truth. It feels more real than everyday waking reality. >> Right. So, when Boothby realized love conquers all, the noetic quality bypassed his rational filters and planted it as absolute truth. The third mark is transiency. The acute peak doesn't last long, just a few hours, but the psychological traces persist for a lifetime. And the fourth mark is passivity. The sensation that your own individual will is suspended. The ego is no longer the author of the experience. >> You feel as though you're being held by a superior external power. These four marks highlight the core paradox here. Mhm. How can a material chemical, a synthetic molecule, reliably induce an unshakable belief in a non-material spiritual reality? When researchers put people on psychedelics into fMRI machines, they expected to see the brain light up with increased activity, but they found the exact opposite. Psilocybin profoundly decreases blood flow in a specific network called the default mode network or DMN. The DMN is essentially the neurological seat of the ego. >> Right. Exactly. It handles self-reflection, autobiographical memory, worrying about the future, and enforcing our psychological defense mechanisms. Psilocybin perfectly mimics serotonin and binds to the receptors, temporarily taking the DMN offline. So, when the DMN is suppressed, the ego functionally disappears. The rigid boundary between self and other vanishes, which explains those profound feelings of unity. Roland Griffiths wrestles with this from a materialist viewpoint. Is it just a neurochemical illusion? He maintains a stance of scientific agnosticism. Whether it's a true spiritual dimension or a brain glitch, science finally has a tool to trigger and study these peak states. The Hopkins trial successfully validated the clinical application using synthetic pills in curated rooms. But this molecule doesn't originate in the lab. To grasp its full scope, we have to shift from the sterile clinic back to the dirt. We have to explore the fungal perspective. And there is no better guide for navigating the fungal kingdom than Paul Stamets. Stamets is a fascinating, almost mythological figure. He's a largely uncredentialed mycologist, operates entirely outside traditional academia, yet he's considered one of the foremost experts on fungi in the world. He holds dozens of patents, consults for the Department of Defense, and literally authored the definitive scientific field guide on psilocybin mushrooms. And he famously wears a hat made entirely out of amadou. The amadou hat is a perfect physical representation of Stamets. Amadou is a spongy material derived from a hoof fungus. It's the exact same fungal material that Ötzi the Iceman, the 5,000-year-old mummy, was carrying to start fires. His personal entry into mycology is like a superhero origin story centered on neuroplasticity. During college, Stamets suffered from a severe, debilitating stutter. It crippled his social life. Driven by curiosity, he decided to consume a massive dose of wild magic mushrooms, around 10 g, which is a heroic, frankly dangerous amount for a novice. >> And a huge thunderstorm rolls in while he's wandering in the woods. Seeking shelter, he climbs to the top of a large oak tree. Trapped at the top of this tree, tripping intensely, surrounded by lightning, he's convinced he's going to be electrocuted. But the psilocybin induced a moment of hyper-lucidity. He realized he needed to fundamentally change his life. He focused all his cognitive energy on his stutter. He just kept repeating, "Stop stuttering now. Stop stuttering now." He used the massive influx of psilocybin, which drastically increases neuroplasticity, to reprogram his own speech centers. The next morning, he climbed down, walked past a woman he'd been too terrified to speak to, and said, "Good morning." without a single hesitation. The stutter was permanently gone. That self-healing ignited a lifelong dedication to studying mushrooms. And his work in mycoremediation is revolutionary. He views fungi as the biochemical architects of the ecosystem. He demonstrated that if you inoculate toxic soil contaminated by crude oil with oyster mushroom spores, the mycelium will aggressively digest the pollution. >> They break down the toxic hydrocarbons and turn it into arable soil. He also patented a mutant strain of cordyceps fungus as a natural biopesticide against carpenter ants. He approaches biology like an alchemist. But if we're looking for psilocybin mushrooms in the wild, Stamets established the Stametsian rule for field identification. If a gilled mushroom produces a purplish-brown or black spore print, and the flesh rapidly bruises blue when handled, it is almost certainly psilocybin. That blue bruising is the psilocin compound oxidizing upon contact with the air. Exactly. It's a visual indicator of potency. In the source material, the author goes foraging with Stamets in the Pacific Northwest for Psilocybe azurescens, the most potent species. What's revealing is where they find it. Stamets emphasizes they are organisms of the ecological edge. You don't find them deep in pristine old-growth forests, you find them thriving in environments that have been violently disturbed by humans. Piles of wood chips, logging trails, even manicured suburban parks. It's as if they deliberately follow human debris. Which points to this larger underground intelligence. Stamets describes the mycelial network as the Earth's internet. It's not just a metaphor. Mycorrhizal networks physically connect root systems across forests. They route nutrients to saplings and transmit chemical warning signals about insect attacks. It's a massive decentralized intelligence. Stamets embodies this older tradition of the romantic scientist, like Alexander von Humboldt. They believed strict reductionist science isn't enough. You have to integrate imagination and intuition. A romantic scientist attempts the radical imaginative leap of viewing the world through the plant's eye view or the fungus's eye view, treating nature as a dynamic web of interacting subjectivities. >> Which leads us directly to the most profound evolutionary mystery of this whole exploration. Why did a fungus, millions of years before humans existed, evolve to produce a chemical that perfectly mimics human serotonin? It's a fierce debate. The conservative assumption is that it evolved as a defense mechanism, like nicotine or caffeine, to poison or deter insects. >> Well, chemist Michael Bog argues against that. He points out that if it were a defense mechanism, the fungus would concentrate the chemical in the mycelium, its vital root system. But it concentrates it in the mushroom cap. The exact part designed to be eaten so spores can be dispersed. This supports the spore dispersal theory. Animals eat them, enjoy the altered state, wander off, and excrete the spores miles away. >> It's a biochemical trade for transportation. But then we have Terence McKenna's stone ape theory. He suggested early hominids ate these mushrooms inducing synesthesia which catalyzed the development of human language and consciousness. It's a beautifully poetic narrative, but biologically it's highly problematic. Acquired traits from a drug experience aren't passed down through DNA. Exactly. I have to push back heavily on McKenna here. The mushroom isn't rewriting the genetic code of the ape. But if we discard that, we have Giorgio Samorini's deep patterning theory which is much more plausible. Samorini theorizes that during environmental crises species need to break rigid habits to survive. Psychedelics act as a deep patterning factor inducing radical novel behaviors that might help the group survive the bottleneck. But even with that, I find myself skeptical of the intentionality Stamets assigns to the fungus. He thinks the mushrooms are literally calling out to humanity to save the ecosystem. Yeah. Isn't that just projecting human intention onto a biochemical accident? >> From a reductionist perspective, yes. But look at the macro level result. This simple fungus managed to produce a molecule that alters the consciousness of the most dominant bipedal species on the planet. And we responded by cultivating and distributing its genetics globally on an industrial scale. It's the ultimate biological hack. Blind evolution or intentional strategy, it worked flawlessly. This brings us to the final leg of our exploration. How does this neurochemical deep patterning actually feel outside the clinic? The author details consuming the Azurescens mushrooms with his wife Judith. Her reaction shows the variability of these compounds. She becomes intensely uncomfortable, gripped by social anxiety, and retreats indoors. It amplifies whatever is present, but the author stays outside in his garden. As his DMN is suppressed, he experiences the objective correlative. Where internal emotional states superimpose onto external physical objects, the environment reflects your deepest psychological realities. He looks at a climbing hydrangea and stripped of his categorizing algorithms, he experiences a plant's eye view. He feels the plant is looking back at him with ancient curiosity. He's directly experiencing animism, the palpable personhood of the plant. Then he looks at two large trees in his yard, a solid ash and an elegant leaning oak with a deep rot at its base. In this deep pattern state, he has an undeniable realization. These two trees are his parents. The ash is his father, the oak is his mother, and the rot connects with his suppressed anxiety about their mortality. >> He is flooded by pure, unadulterated confrontation with grief. A skeptic would say he just hallucinated, but psychologically he saw the exact same trees that existed in reality. The psilocybin just altered the lens of perception. >> It stripped away the ego's filtering mechanism, restoring a child's eye view. Nature revealed itself as fully alive and interconnected. The illusion was his everyday sober perception that he was separate from nature. The profound takeaway is that you don't necessarily need a drug to access the state. We are fundamentally discussing the psychology of awe. The mystical realm is right here in your backyard, hidden just behind our everyday mental habits. This deep dive has traced an extraordinary arc from Hoffman's 1943 bicycle ride to the repressed 1950 science, Bob Jesse's 2006 Hopkins Renaissance, and Stamets' mycelial networks. As we conclude, consider this. Billions in venture capital are flooding into psychedelics. Corporations are trying to patent and commodify a mystical experience fungi have provided for free. Will it alter society or just become another expensive pharmaceutical? That is the ultimate tension moving forward. So, today as you disconnect, look at the trees and the mental algorithms organizing your mind. View them with a slightly unpatterned perspective. You might discover the awe you're looking for is already right in front of you. Thanks for joining us on this deep dive.

I thought they were really scary substances. I never used them when I was young. I had absorbed all the kind of propaganda that these were destroyers of young minds, that you would have a trip that you'd never come back from. So, it was a kind of a surprise and a revelation to me when I learned that this scary substance that I'd been avoiding my whole life was being used to treat mental illness, not create it. Hello and welcome to the Life Lessons podcast with me, Simon Mundy. This podcast has a simple mission to have discussions that reveal something important about life and how best to live it. My guests range from the biggest sporting names on the planet through to neuroscientists, philosophers, psychologists, and world-renowned thinkers. We talk about things like how to skillfully relate to uncomfortable thoughts and feelings, the power of acceptance and psychological flexibility, how to get your circadian rhythms in sync to feel your best, right through to the nature of reality. These conversations and the bite-size episodes have the power to change your life. 2023 has been described as the year of psychedelics, and as research into the incredible healing powers of substances such as magic mushrooms gains pace, I am thrilled to be joined by Michael Pollan, the author and journalist described as one of the 100 most influential people in the world by Time magazine. He is right at the forefront of the psychedelic renaissance. Michael's written eight books, primarily about our relationship with nature and food, but more recently he's headed into the realm of psychedelics and other mind-altering substances, including coffee. In How to Change Your Mind, Michael researches how psychedelics, including psilocybin mushrooms, are being used to help people suffering from various mental health conditions, including depression, anxiety, PTSD, and addiction. The results have been staggering. That book has now been turned into a four-part Netflix series of the same name, and it is highly recommended viewing. In February, Australia became the first country to officially recognize psychedelics as medicines, acknowledging that substances like magic mushrooms could help treatment-resistant conditions. The same is now true in a number of American states. So, psychedelic research is clearly a growing and interesting area, and has the potential to help a lot of people who are suffering. That said, psychedelics are not to be taken lightly. They are extremely powerful substances that must be treated with respect. And that's why the work that is taking place in various universities and medical institutions around the world to explore how people can take part in psychedelic therapy safely is so important. In this episode, Michael and I explore how his books and Netflix series have played a pivotal role in changing how psychedelics are seen, as well as their potential to improve people's mental health and well-being, and how the worlds of science, medicine, and psychiatry are increasingly embracing psychedelics as a potential treatment. Uh Charles II tried to ban the coffee houses cuz he he didn't like the kind of conversation that was happening there. It was a very political uh conversation. So, there's always a set of kind of socio-political reasons why one drug is celebrated and the other is not. I think that there's a certain kind of economic determinism in this. I think certain drugs um grease the wheels of of the machine of society, of capitalism. And and and coffee is and tea are definitely in that category. They make us better workers. And and, you know, so it's not surprising that these are celebrated in our culture. And then there are other drugs that are seen as as disruptive in one way or another. Um you know, cannabis is changing its identity in the US, but that was a big part of the drug war for many years. And people, you know, on cannabis don't work very hard. We're not doing therapeutic work. Uh we're going to be really trying to understand the uh the mechanisms uh by which psychedelics work. We have some very powerful fMRI technology that hasn't been used in the field ever before. Uh so we're going to get a better sense of what's going on in the brain when people have a psychedelic experience. And we're studying perception and and belief change and things like that. Um but we're doing two other interesting things, too. We're training guides. Uh we just started our first cohort this fall. It's interesting to note that in history whatever the cool cutting edge technology was brains were likened to that. So, it was it was looms for a while, it was uh clocks for a while, it was telephone switchboards. Whatever was the cool technology, surely that's what that's how brains work. Now, it's computers. But think about it. In a computer, you have this sharp distinction between hardware and software. That's the key to their success, and you can run the same program on any number of different hardwares. They're interchangeable. Brains aren't like that. There's no distinction between hardware and software. Every experience you have, every memory, is a physical change to the brain, to the way it's wired. Um you know, we start out with all these connections and then they get pruned as we grow up. Uh every brain is shaped by its experience. So, this idea that you could separate that consciousness is some kind of software that you could run on other things besides um meat, um I just think doesn't hold up. Is that, um, the research that I found most persuasive about consciousness is, uh, uh, basically has consciousness beginning with feelings, not thoughts. In other words, it's embodied. And I have to just develop this a little bit, um, but we, you know, the brain exists to keep the body alive, not the other way around. Although we tend, since we identify with our heads, where most of our senses are, we we lose track of that. And the body speaks to the brain in feelings, right? You know, feelings of hunger, itchiness, warmth, cold, um, but also feelings of shame, uh, when our social standing is not, you know, has been damaged. Um, anyway, we have these feelings, they depend on a body. Um, feelings have no weight if you're not vulnerable, your body isn't vulnerable. Um, and probably mortal. Um, so consciousness is embodied in a really critical way. And computers are not. Now, robots will be, and I actually fought I interview a guy a a scientist at USC who is trying to make a vulnerable robot. So, he's essentially upholstering the thing with skin that can tear and be damaged. And he's filling the skin with all these sensors so that it can be like us and be vulnerable and and generate feelings that are how consciousness begins. So, for a long time we thought consciousness had to be in the cortex, right? The the most human, newest part of the brain, the outer covering. And that's where rational thought and executive function are and all these kind of things. Um but as it turns out, it really begins with feelings in the brain stem. Let's say you have a feeling of hunger, it registers in the upper brain stem and only later as the cortex get involved like helping you figure out how are you going to feed yourself like imagining, you know, a meal counterfactuals of different meals or making a reservation at a restaurant. All all those are cortical things, but it begins in the brain stem with feelings. So if that is true and I find that really persuasive because people born without a cortex are still conscious. Uh animals that you take the cortex out still show signs of consciousness. Um whereas if you damage the upper brain stem um you're out, you know, you're you're unconscious. So if this is true and consciousness is this embodied phenomenon that depends on having a body to mean anything. Um I don't see how machines are going to do that. But isn't the keyword there if? There is no experience more familiar than what it feels like to be ourselves. No matter what we might be doing, it always seems like something just to be alive and awake. So, how is it possible that so much about consciousness is still a mystery? From KERA in Dallas, this is Think. I'm Krys Boyd. If there was a low-hanging fruit to be picked in studying consciousness, it was figuring out how the brain works, how neurons interact, which ones go to work when we're recalling a memory, or having a conversation, or trying to solve a problem. The why of it all, why should it be those brain processes somehow result in a particular sense of feeling like an individual person? That is the hard problem of consciousness, and it will probably never be solved under a microscope or in a scanner. Challenging as they are, these questions are irresistible to curious minds. So, it makes sense that writer Michael Pollan has taken this on for his most recent project. It's a book called A World Appears, A Journey into Consciousness.
