Um and if I didn't have a sense of self, if I didn't think I would be the same person when the book came out that I was when I agreed to write it, I would never finish it. Um but I know that there's some value to that self that I get this book written and you know, sell a few copies. Um but that continuity may be illusory. I'm not sure. But the idea that you can separate the two is very interesting and there is a a uh a uh philosopher I interviewed uh named Thomas Metzinger who studies this question. He writes about the self. Brilliant uh philosopher. And um he has collected like a 1,500 accounts of people who transcended their sense of self. Usually in meditation, sometimes in psychedelics, uh experiences of great awe in nature would be another way. And sometimes just spontaneously. But he also points out we we have an experience every day of this um of being conscious without having a self. And that is the half second when we wake up in the morning. And it's and it's longer if you're in a hotel room in a strange place where you you can kind of like who am I? Where am I? And you and you and you very quickly it reconsolidates. But you start from this baseline of just being conscious without this self. I've read child development experts who believe that to a certain extent the self requires learning, right? Because babies, in the same way that we don't recall this, but they have to learn how to, you know, visually process the information entering their eyes and orally process what's coming in through their ears, they have to learn that they are not the same person, the same self as whoever is caring for them. >> Or their mothers who, you know, they were physically a part of that self. They were not separate selves, we're not born as separate selves. The umbilical cord is cut and we start this journey to and we don't have a sense of our separateness till we're a couple years old. Um and that's kind of what we're acquiring. And then around age five, we get episodic memory, the ability to string together a self based on what happened in the past. Um but that doesn't kick in. That's one of the reasons you don't remember anything when you're younger than five or or rarely do you have memories of that period because you haven't learned how to lay down those memories yet. And um so yeah, becoming a self is a process and we don't start out that way. There's a lot we still don't understand about how anesthesia works, but we do know anyone who's experienced it knows it it's different than falling asleep, right? Where you even if you're kind of dead to the world, you're aware of the passage of time when you wake up, maybe you're dreaming and your mind is active. As far as most people know, when we are um under anesthesia, we're not having any kind of of thoughts. I mean, is there anything we can learn about that kind of synthetic unconsciousness that can teach us about consciousness? Well, there is some work to determine whether brains are conscious, uh especially when people are in a vegetative state. You know, they're they're non-responsive, they've had a serious injury. And families are deciding whether to, you know, pull the plug essentially or hope that this person will come back. And it turns out that about 20% of people in that state are conscious, which is a terrifying thought because they can't communicate that they're conscious. They can't even blink their eyes in a pattern to indicate their conscious. Um, but some of them wake up again and and we learn that they were they heard everything that was going on in the room and couldn't do anything about it. And um, uh, so Kristof Koch, one of the scientists that I profile in the book, is actually been working on a machine that could measure consciousness and and identify those people. And it has to do with passing magnetic waves through the brain and looking at the kinds of how they get reflected back. It's uh, I think it's trying to measure brain waves, um, which is more reliable than just fMRI imagery. So, that's um, and anesthesia, you're right, is very different than sleep. Um, there are phases in which you are conscious during sleep. Dreaming is a is a form of consciousness. Um, there are also periods of deep sleep where you're not conscious. Um, but um, that's why that that this question about plants being anesthetized so interesting. If you have these two modes of existence of of ways of being, it implies that one of them is awake and and and that is implies a kind of consciousness, which I think would be very hard for mainstream science to accept right away. Um, everything about anesthesia is a mystery though. If you talk to anesthesiologist, you realize the science is not really well understood. And something like xenon gas, which is completely inert, so there's no chemical reaction going on, nevertheless puts us out completely and puts the plants out. So, there's much to be learned about anesthesia. You share in the book, Michael, that there has been exactly one time in your entire life when you had the experience of complete ego-lessness. Tell us about that day. Yeah, so this So, you know, uh one or two books ago, I was working on psychedelics and psychedelic therapy, and I wrote a book called How to Change Your Mind. And, um you know, the way I do journalism is participatory, um often when I was writing about the cattle industry, I bought a cow and followed her through the process. And, uh to write about architecture, I built a building. And, I like I like finding that place in the middle of a story to tell it in the first person, because you get um you get an access you never get just by interviewing people. Uh and, you have the wonder of doing something for the first time. So, I experimented with psychedelics, and in one of my experiments, I was working with a guide and took a fairly high dose of psilocybin. And, at the climax of that experience, which had many weird episodes, um I experienced myself, my sense of self, exploding into a cloud of blue Post-it notes. And, uh and then they kind of, you know, fluttered to the ground like snow. And, when they hit the ground, they turned into a pool of blue paint. And, I looked at that. Now, I know the pronouns are hard to get right, but something looked at that, and I realized that was me. And, I was no longer, you know, the self was gone. Yet, I was still conscious. It wasn't troubling at all. It seemed just right that being a pool of blue paint was what you should be. And, um but, I had no self. And, now this can be a very scary experience if you fight to hold on to your ego, but if you're willing to surrender, which is a very important principle of guided psychedelic therapy, um it's fine. And uh consciousness goes on, and I thought that was significant. Um what happens though that I found most interesting is the self is about separateness, right? The ego is a defensive structure. But when those walls come down, there's nothing between you and whatever the larger universe is. So you have this sense of merging with something bigger than yourselves. And that might be for religiously inclined people the div- you know, the divine. Um it might be nature. It might be In my case it was a piece of music. And the guide was playing this um Bach uh cello suite, unaccompanied cello suite. And um uh I heard this piece of music in a new way, um which is to say I wasn't a subject listening to the object of the music. There was no distinction between subject and object. I became this piece of music. I could not distinguish myself from it. It just was in me, I was in it. It was the most profound experience of music I'd ever had. And that's because that subject-object division that selfhood uh enforces was gone. Um and it it felt it was a won- it was a very sad piece of music, but it felt wonderful. Um I felt more connected to something outside myself than I had ever felt. You talk about letting go into not knowing. Um and it sounds like it in in that case it was a positive experience for you. It's an interesting thing to discover about yourself given that you've spent your entire career trying to know more about much anything you set your mind to. So, you know, I approached consciousness the way any journalist approaches any subject, which is, you know, questions and answers. You got these questions, you're going to you're on a hunt for answers. Um and also with this very Western male, you know, problem-solution frame. And um and I developed this very narrow focus. Just I was so intent on like finding the theory of consciousness that I could endorse and and that would explain it to me. And by the way, there are like more than there are 22 theories of consciousness and by one count, 200 by another count. So, there's a they're flailing out there. Um and uh and I and I was getting kind of frustrated. Um and then I had this kind of turn. Um some of it helped by my wife, who's an artist. And she would say, you know, there's there's some very positive things about not knowing. Um you know, you it opens you up to possibilities and imagination and, you know, very much an artist's way of looking at it. And I'm like, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, but I'm a journalist and I want answers. And then I reached out uh when I was working on the self to a Zen teacher named Joan Halifax, who has a retreat center in Santa Fe. And she's a very wise woman. She's in her 80s. She's um uh and I wanted to talk to her about the self cuz she had told me her Zen retreat center was a factory for the deconstruction of selves. And I wanted to try that and see what that was about. But in the end and I should have known this that a Zen teacher was not going to help me intellectualize this problem. Um she thought I was lost in my head and said, you know, instead of talking to you all about self, I think you should go to the cave. and she dispatched me to these caves on her property. Um, and this is way up in the mountains north of Santa Fe. And it was very kind of simple existence. Um, no running water, no electricity, middle of nowhere, absolute quiet. And I went there to think about the self and think about consciousness. And what I and I meditated for hours every day, and I had a very ritualized existence of, you know, chopping wood and sweeping the threshold of my little cave. It was a very interesting experience. But, um, I I came to see that yes, there is the problem of consciousness, but then there's the fact of it, that the marvelous amazing fact that we have this space of interiority where we were ultimately private, and we can think whatever we want. It's a space of mental freedom. And I wasn't using it. I was I was narrowing my, um, my my attention in such a way that the, um, I was less conscious than I could have been. And it made me realize we're all less conscious than we could be, and that we fill our heads with, you know, scrolling on social media and now interacting with chatbots. And all these things are kind of colonizing this this precious space. And that, um, you know, we assume we're more conscious than animals, but actually we're less conscious than animals. Because they have to be present all the time or they're at at risk. And because of the luxuries of our technological civilization, we have a sense of safety, and that allows us to to retreat from consciousness. And so, my focus shifted to, um, appreciating this gift, um, which many of us don't think about very often at all. And figuring out how how could I be more conscious? How could I keep that space? How could I defend it from all the companies that want to monetize it? Um, you know, hacking our attention with social media. Um, we also have a president who's very good at taking over our consciousness for hours every day for many of us, especially for journalists. Um, so that was a kind of shift in my thinking and it was one I did not expect when I started on this project. Michael, thanks so much for making time to talk. >> Oh, thanks, Chris. It was a great pleasure talking to you. Think is distributed by PRX, the Public Radio Exchange. You can find us on Facebook, Instagram, anywhere you get podcasts, and at think.kera.org. Again, I'm Krys Boyd. Thanks for listening. Have a great day.
